facts: Born on September 21, 1947, in Portland, Maine, and raised primarily by his mother after his father abandoned the family when he was two years old.
Published his first professional short story, 'The Glass Floor,' in 1967 while still in college.
Wrote the novel 'Carrie' in 1974, which became his first published novel and a major success, launching his career as a full-time writer.
Has published over 60 novels and 200 short stories, many of which have been adapted into films, television series, and comics.
Used the pseudonym Richard Bachman to publish several novels, including 'Rage' (1977) and 'The Running Man' (1982), to test whether his success was due to talent or luck.
Suffered a major accident in 1999 when he was struck by a van while walking near his home, leading to multiple surgeries and a long recovery period.
Wrote the non-fiction book 'On Writing: A Memoir of the Craft' in 2000, offering insights into his life and writing process.
Received the National Medal of Arts from the U.S. National Endowment for the Arts in 2015 for his contributions to literature.
Known for his prolific output, often writing thousands of words per day and maintaining a disciplined writing schedule.
Created the fictional town of Castle Rock, Maine, which serves as the setting for many of his stories, including 'Cujo' and 'Needful Things'.
Won numerous awards, including the Bram Stoker Award, the World Fantasy Award, and the British Fantasy Society Award.
His novel 'The Green Mile' was originally published in six serial installments in 1996 before being released as a single volume.
Collaborated with his son, Joe Hill, on the comic book series 'Road Rage' in 2012, marking one of their few joint projects.
Has been open about his struggles with alcoholism and drug addiction during the 1980s, which he overcame in the late 1980s.
Established the Stephen and Tabitha King Foundation, which provides grants to communities in Maine for education, the arts, and social services.

image.commons_file: Stephen King at the 2024 Toronto International Film Festival 2 (cropped).jpg
image.author: Kevin Payravi

summary.fr: Cet écrivain américain est célèbre pour ses romans d'horreur et de suspense, publiés depuis les années 1970. Il a marqué la littérature avec des œuvres comme *Carrie* ou *Shining*, souvent adaptées au cinéma. Connu pour sa productivité exceptionnelle, il a reçu de nombreux prix pour son influence dans le genre fantastique.
summary.en: This American author is renowned for his horror and suspense novels, published since the 1970s. He shaped modern literature with works like *Carrie* and *The Shining*, many of which became iconic films. A prolific writer, he has won multiple awards for his contributions to the fantasy and horror genres.

name: Audrey Hepburn
facts: Born on May 4, 1929, in Brussels, Belgium, to an English father and Dutch mother.
Spent part of childhood in the Netherlands during World War II, enduring hardship and malnutrition.
Trained as a ballet dancer before transitioning to acting due to financial constraints and physical limitations post-war.
Made her film debut in 1951's *One Wild Oat* but gained fame with *Roman Holiday* (1953), winning an Academy Award for Best Actress.
Became an international icon for her role as Holly Golightly in *Breakfast at Tiffany's* (1961).
Starred in *My Fair Lady* (1964), though her singing was dubbed by Marni Nixon due to studio concerns.
Won a Tony Award for Best Actress in a Play for her performance in *Ondine* (1954) on Broadway.
Nominated for an Academy Award five times, winning once for *Roman Holiday*.
Known for her distinctive style, often collaborating with designer Hubert de Givenchy, who created her iconic little black dress in *Breakfast at Tiffany's*.
Retired from acting in the late 1960s to focus on family and humanitarian work.
Served as a Goodwill Ambassador for UNICEF from 1988 until her death, traveling to impoverished regions in Africa, Asia, and Latin America.
Awarded the Presidential Medal of Freedom in 1992 for her humanitarian efforts.
Diagnosed with appendiceal cancer in 1992 and passed away on January 20, 1993, at the age of 63 in Tolochenaz, Switzerland.
Posthumously awarded the Jean Hersholt Humanitarian Award by the Academy of Motion Picture Arts and Sciences in 1993.
Remains a fashion and cultural icon, frequently ranked among the greatest female screen legends of all time.

summary.fr: Cette actrice britannique, née en 1929, est devenue une icône du cinéma grâce à son rôle dans *Vacances romaines*, pour lequel elle a remporté un Oscar. Elle est aussi célèbre pour son élégance et son engagement humanitaire avec l'UNICEF. Son style intemporel et son talent ont marqué l'âge d'or d'Hollywood.
summary.en: This British actress, born in 1929, became a cinema icon after winning an Oscar for her role in *Roman Holiday*. She is also known for her timeless style and later work as a UNICEF Goodwill Ambassador. Her performances defined classic Hollywood and left a lasting legacy in film and humanitarian efforts.

name: Scarlett Johansson
facts: Born on November 22, 1984, in New York City, to a Danish father and an American mother of Polish-Jewish descent.
Made her film debut at age 9 in the 1994 fantasy comedy *North* and gained early recognition for *The Horse Whisperer* (1998).
Rose to international fame with her role as Charlotte in *Lost in Translation* (2003), earning a BAFTA Award for Best Actress.
Became one of Hollywood’s highest-paid actresses, earning over $20 million for films like *The Avengers* (2012) and its sequels.
Portrayed Black Widow (Natasha Romanoff) in nine Marvel Cinematic Universe films, starting with *Iron Man 2* (2010).
Won a Tony Award in 2010 for Best Featured Actress in a Play for her role in *A View from the Bridge* on Broadway.
Received two Academy Award nominations: Best Actress for *Marriage Story* (2019) and Best Supporting Actress for *Jojo Rabbit* (2019).
Voiced the AI character Samantha in the 2013 sci-fi film *Her*, earning critical acclaim for her vocal performance.
Starred in *Match Point* (2005), directed by Woody Allen, which marked the beginning of their frequent collaborations.
Founded the production company *These Pictures* in 2017 to develop and produce film and television projects.
Advocated for gender pay equality in Hollywood, publicly addressing disparities in compensation for female actors.
Married Canadian actor Ryan Reynolds in 2008, divorced in 2011, and later married French journalist Romain Dauriac in 2014.
Has two children: a daughter born in 2014 and a son born in 2021 with her current husband, comedian Colin Jost.
Holds dual American and Danish citizenship due to her father’s heritage.
Ranked as one of *Forbes*’ highest-earning actresses multiple times, including a peak of $56 million in 2019.

summary.fr: Cette actrice américaine est née en 1984 à New York et est devenue célèbre grâce à ses rôles dans des films comme *Lost in Translation* et *Match Point*. Elle est aussi connue pour avoir incarné Black Widow dans les films Marvel et pour son engagement en faveur de l'égalité salariale à Hollywood. Son talent lui a valu des nominations aux Oscars et un Tony Award.
summary.en: This American actress, born in 1984 in New York, rose to fame with roles in films like *Lost in Translation* and *Match Point*. She is best known for playing Black Widow in Marvel movies and advocating for gender pay equality in Hollywood. Her work has earned her Oscar nominations and a Tony Award.

name: Keanu Reeves
facts: Born on September 2, 1964, in Beirut, Lebanon, to an English mother and a Hawaiian father of Chinese, English, Irish, and Portuguese descent.
Moved frequently during childhood, living in Australia, New York, and Toronto, where he eventually settled.
Began acting in theater productions and television films in Toronto before transitioning to Hollywood.
Gained widespread recognition for the 1989 comedy film *Bill & Ted’s Excellent Adventure* and its 1991 sequel.
Starred as the titular character in *Point Break* (1991), solidifying his action-hero persona.
Achieved global fame as Neo in *The Matrix* trilogy (1999–2003), a groundbreaking sci-fi franchise.
Played the iconic role of John Wick in the eponymous action film series, starting in 2014.
Co-founded the production company Company Films in 2012, which has produced several of his projects.
Known for performing many of his own stunts, including fight scenes and motorcycle sequences.
Donated a significant portion of his *Matrix* salary to cancer research and children’s hospitals.
Rode a motorcycle across South America in the early 2000s, documenting the journey in a book titled *Ode to Happiness*.
Voiced the character Duke Caboom in the 2019 animated film *Toy Story 4*.
Received a star on the Hollywood Walk of Fame in 2005 for his contributions to the film industry.
Known for his humility and generosity, often giving gifts to crew members and supporting various charities anonymously.
Published a modern adaptation of *Prince Valiant* comics in 2017, titled *BRZRKR*, which he co-wrote.

summary.fr: Cet acteur canadien est célèbre pour ses rôles dans des films d'action et de science-fiction. Il est notamment connu pour avoir incarné Neo dans la trilogie *Matrix* et John Wick dans la série de films du même nom. Sa carrière à Hollywood a débuté dans les années 1980, et il est apprécié pour ses cascades impressionnantes.
summary.en: This Canadian actor is famous for his roles in action and sci-fi films. He is best known for playing Neo in *The Matrix* trilogy and John Wick in the action film series. His Hollywood career began in the 1980s, and he is admired for performing his own stunts.

name: Robert De Niro
facts: Born on August 17, 1943, in New York City to artists Virginia Admiral and Robert De Niro Sr.
Studied acting at the Stella Adler Conservatory and the Lee Strasberg Theatre and Film Institute.
Made a film debut at age 20 in Brian De Palma's 1963 film *The Wedding Party*, though it was released in 1969.
Gained critical acclaim for the role of Vito Corleone in *The Godfather Part II* (1974), winning an Academy Award for Best Supporting Actor.
Won a second Academy Award for Best Actor for his portrayal of Jake LaMotta in *Raging Bull* (1980).
Co-founded the Tribeca Film Festival in 2002 to help revitalize Lower Manhattan after the 9/11 attacks.
Known for his method acting, famously gaining 60 pounds and learning to box for *Raging Bull*.
Collaborated with director Martin Scorsese on nine films, including *Taxi Driver* (1976), *Goodfellas* (1990), and *The Irishman* (2019).
Starred in *Taxi Driver* (1976) as Travis Bickle, delivering the iconic line, 'You talkin' to me?'
Owns the Nobu restaurant chain and the Greenwich Hotel in Tribeca, New York.
Played a memorable role as a troubled veteran in *The Deer Hunter* (1978), which won the Academy Award for Best Picture.
Directed and starred in *A Bronx Tale* (1993), his directorial debut, based on a one-man play by Chazz Palminteri.
Received the Presidential Medal of Freedom from President Barack Obama in 2016.
Appeared in over 130 films, spanning a career of more than five decades.
Known for his intense and often intimidating on-screen presence, particularly in crime dramas and thrillers.

summary.fr: Cet acteur américain, né en 1943, est une légende du cinéma. Il a remporté deux Oscars pour ses rôles dans *Raging Bull* et *Le Parrain 2*. Connu pour son travail avec Martin Scorsese, il a aussi cofondé le Festival du film de Tribeca.
summary.en: This American actor, born in 1943, is a cinema icon. He won two Academy Awards for *Raging Bull* and *The Godfather Part II*. Famous for his collaborations with Martin Scorsese, he also co-founded the Tribeca Film Festival.

name: Kendrick Lamar
facts: Born on June 17, 1987, in Compton, California, a city known for its influence on hip-hop culture.
Began writing rhymes at the age of 8, inspired by Tupac Shakur and Dr. Dre’s 1992 album *The Chronic*.
Released his first full-length project, *Section.80*, independently in 2011, which gained critical acclaim.
Signed to Dr. Dre’s Aftermath Entertainment and Top Dawg Entertainment (TDE) in 2012.
Won a Pulitzer Prize for Music in 2018 for the album *DAMN.*, becoming the first non-classical or jazz artist to receive the honor.
Released the critically acclaimed album *good kid, m.A.A.d city* in 2012, which is often regarded as a modern hip-hop classic.
Collaborated with artists like SZA, Jay Rock, and Schoolboy Q as part of the hip-hop collective Black Hippy.
Known for intricate lyricism, storytelling, and conceptual albums that explore themes of race, identity, and personal growth.
Performed at the 2016 Grammy Awards with a politically charged performance featuring chains, prison imagery, and a fiery backdrop.
Released *To Pimp a Butterfly* in 2015, an album praised for its jazz-infused sound and exploration of African-American culture.
Won 13 Grammy Awards out of 37 nominations, including Best Rap Album for *DAMN.* and *To Pimp a Butterfly*.
Featured on the soundtrack of *Black Panther* (2018), contributing the single *All the Stars* with SZA.
Known for his low-key personal life, rarely granting interviews and maintaining a private social media presence.
Performed at the Coachella Valley Music and Arts Festival in 2017 and 2018, with the latter being a historic headlining set.
Often incorporates spoken word and poetry into his music, reflecting influences from artists like Gil Scott-Heron.

summary.fr: Ce rappeur américain est né en 1987 à Compton, une ville emblématique du hip-hop. Il est célèbre pour ses albums conceptuels comme *DAMN.* et *To Pimp a Butterfly*, qui explorent des thèmes sociaux et personnels. En 2018, il est devenu le premier artiste non classique ou jazz à remporter un prix Pulitzer pour la musique.
summary.en: This American rapper was born in 1987 in Compton, a city central to hip-hop culture. He is known for his storytelling and albums like *DAMN.* and *To Pimp a Butterfly*, which tackle race and identity. In 2018, he became the first non-classical or jazz artist to win a Pulitzer Prize for music.

name: Ariana Grande
facts: Born on June 26, 1993, in Boca Raton, Florida, to a family of Italian descent.
Began her career in Broadway musical *13* at the age of 15 before rising to fame on Nickelodeon's *Victorious* in 2009.
Released her debut album, *Yours Truly*, in 2013, which debuted at number one on the US Billboard 200.
Holds the record for the most top 10 debuts on the Billboard Hot 100 by a female artist, with 19 entries as of 2023.
Won her first Grammy Award in 2019 for Best Pop Vocal Album for *Sweetener*.
Known for her four-octave vocal range and signature whistle notes, often compared to Mariah Carey.
Her 2019 album *Thank U, Next* broke streaming records, with the title track becoming a cultural phenomenon.
Headlined Coachella in 2019, making her the youngest artist and fourth female headliner in the festival's history.
Suffered a traumatic brain injury and PTSD after the Manchester Arena bombing during her *Dangerous Woman Tour* in 2017, which killed 22 fans.
Organized the *One Love Manchester* benefit concert in 2017, raising over £17 million for victims of the attack.
Has collaborated with artists like Lady Gaga, Justin Bieber, The Weeknd, and Nicki Minaj, among others.
Her 2020 album *Positions* debuted at number one on the Billboard 200, making her the first artist to have five number-one debuts in the 2010s.
Starred in the 2021 film adaptation of *Don’t Look Up* alongside Leonardo DiCaprio and Jennifer Lawrence.
Has over 350 million records sold worldwide, making her one of the best-selling music artists of all time.
Advocates for mental health awareness, LGBTQ+ rights, and animal welfare, often using her platform to promote social causes.

summary.fr: Cette chanteuse américaine est l'une des artistes pop les plus influentes des années 2010 et 2020. Connue pour sa voix puissante et son registre vocal impressionnant, elle a débuté comme actrice avant de dominer les charts avec des albums comme *Thank U, Next*. Elle a également marqué l'histoire en organisant un concert caritatif après l'attentat de Manchester en 2017.
summary.en: This American pop singer is one of the most influential artists of the 2010s and 2020s. Famous for her powerful voice and four-octave range, she started as an actress before topping charts with albums like *Thank U, Next*. She also made history by organizing a benefit concert after the 2017 Manchester Arena attack.

name: Rosalind Franklin
facts: Born on July 25, 1920, in Notting Hill, London, into a prominent British Jewish family.
Studied physical chemistry at Newnham College, University of Cambridge, graduating in 1941.
Worked on the structure of coal and carbon during World War II, contributing to the war effort.
Earned a PhD from Cambridge University in 1945 for research on the porosity of coal.
Moved to Paris in 1947 to work at the Laboratoire Central des Services Chimiques de l'État, mastering X-ray diffraction techniques.
Joined King’s College London in 1951 to work on the structure of DNA using X-ray crystallography.
Produced the famous Photo 51, an X-ray diffraction image of DNA that was critical to discovering its double-helix structure.
Her data was shared without her knowledge with James Watson and Francis Crick, who used it to build their DNA model in 1953.
Published key findings on the structure of DNA in the same 1953 issue of *Nature* as Watson and Crick but received less recognition.
Switched focus to studying the structure of viruses, particularly the tobacco mosaic virus, at Birkbeck College in 1953.
Collaborated with Aaron Klug and others to produce groundbreaking research on viral structures using X-ray diffraction.
Diagnosed with ovarian cancer in 1956, continuing her research despite declining health.
Died on April 16, 1958, at the age of 37, from complications related to cancer.
Posthumously recognized for her pivotal contributions to the discovery of DNA’s structure, though she was not awarded a Nobel Prize.
Awarded the Louisa Gross Horwitz Prize posthumously in 2008 for her work on DNA and viruses.

summary.fr: Cette scientifique britannique a joué un rôle clé dans la découverte de la structure de l'ADN grâce à ses travaux en cristallographie aux rayons X. Son célèbre cliché, la Photo 51, a permis à Watson et Crick d'élaborer leur modèle de la double hélice en 1953. Malgré son importance, son apport a longtemps été sous-estimé dans l'histoire des sciences.
summary.en: This British scientist made crucial contributions to uncovering the structure of DNA through her work in X-ray crystallography. Her famous Photo 51 provided essential data that helped Watson and Crick develop their double-helix model in 1953. Despite her pivotal role, her work was often overlooked in the history of science.

name: Niels Bohr
facts: Born on October 7, 1885, in Copenhagen, Denmark, to a family of academics.
Developed the Bohr model of the atom in 1913, introducing the idea of electrons traveling in discrete orbits around the nucleus.
Won the Nobel Prize in Physics in 1922 for investigations into the structure of atoms and the radiation emanating from them.
Played a key role in the development of quantum mechanics, contributing to the Copenhagen interpretation of quantum theory.
Worked closely with Werner Heisenberg and other prominent physicists at the Institute for Theoretical Physics in Copenhagen, later renamed the Niels Bohr Institute.
Assisted many Jewish scientists in escaping Nazi-occupied Europe during World War II, including helping them find refuge in the United States and the UK.
Collaborated on the Manhattan Project during World War II, though he later advocated for international control of nuclear weapons.
Proposed the principle of complementarity, suggesting that objects have pairs of complementary properties that cannot be observed or measured simultaneously.
Published over 100 scientific papers throughout his career, covering topics from atomic structure to nuclear physics.
Received the Atoms for Peace Award in 1957 for his efforts to promote peaceful uses of atomic energy.
Served as the president of the Royal Danish Academy of Sciences and Letters from 1939 until his death in 1962.
His son, Aage Bohr, also won the Nobel Prize in Physics in 1975 for work on the structure of the atomic nucleus.
Died on November 18, 1962, in Copenhagen, Denmark, from heart failure.
The element bohrium (Bh), discovered in 1981, was named in his honor.
Known for his deep philosophical approach to science, often engaging in debates about the nature of reality and quantum theory.

image.author: The American Institute of Physics credits the photo [1] to AB Lagrelius & Westphal, which is the Swedish company used by the Nobel Foundation for most photos of its book series Les Prix Nobel.

summary.fr: Ce scientifique danois du début du XXe siècle a révolutionné la physique en proposant un modèle de l'atome où les électrons gravitent autour du noyau sur des orbites précises. Lauréat du prix Nobel en 1922, il a joué un rôle clé dans le développement de la mécanique quantique et a fondé l'interprétation de Copenhague. Son travail a aussi marqué l'histoire de l'énergie nucléaire et de la diplomatie scientifique.
summary.en: This Danish scientist from the early 20th century transformed physics with his atomic model, showing electrons orbiting the nucleus in fixed paths. Winner of the 1922 Nobel Prize, he was a pioneer of quantum mechanics and the Copenhagen interpretation. His legacy spans nuclear physics, wartime science, and efforts to promote peaceful uses of atomic energy.

name: Max Planck
facts: Born on April 23, 1858, in Kiel, Schleswig, then part of the Danish Duchy of Holstein.
Proposed the quantum theory in 1900, revolutionizing the understanding of atomic and subatomic processes.
Introduced Planck's constant (h), a fundamental physical constant central to quantum mechanics.
Awarded the Nobel Prize in Physics in 1918 for the discovery of energy quanta.
Studied physics at the University of Munich and later at the University of Berlin under renowned physicists like Hermann von Helmholtz.
Served as a professor of theoretical physics at the University of Berlin from 1889 to 1928.
Published the law of black-body radiation, known as Planck's law, which resolved the ultraviolet catastrophe in classical physics.
Authored the book 'The Theory of Heat Radiation,' a seminal work in the field of thermodynamics.
Played a key role in the establishment of the Kaiser Wilhelm Society (now Max Planck Society) in 1911, serving as its president from 1930 to 1937.
Endured personal tragedies, including the loss of his eldest son during World War I and the execution of another son in 1945 for involvement in a plot to assassinate Hitler.
Advocated for the acceptance of Albert Einstein's theory of relativity in Germany during a time of resistance to new scientific ideas.
Received numerous honors, including the Copley Medal from the Royal Society in 1929.
Retired from active research in 1928 but continued to lecture and write on scientific and philosophical topics.
Died on October 4, 1947, in Göttingen, Germany, at the age of 89.
The Max Planck Society, one of Germany's leading research organizations, is named in his honor.

image.author: Courtesy of the Clendening History of Medicine Library, University of Kansas Medical Center.

summary.fr: Ce scientifique allemand, né en 1858, a révolutionné la physique avec sa théorie des quanta en 1900. Il a introduit la constante de Planck, fondamentale en mécanique quantique, et reçu le prix Nobel de physique en 1918 pour ses découvertes. Ses travaux ont marqué le début d’une nouvelle ère dans la compréhension de l’infiniment petit.
summary.en: This German scientist, born in 1858, revolutionized physics with his quantum theory in 1900. He introduced Planck’s constant, a cornerstone of quantum mechanics, and won the Nobel Prize in Physics in 1918. His work laid the foundation for modern atomic and subatomic science.

name: Erwin Schrodinger
facts: Won the Nobel Prize in Physics in 1933 for the formulation of wave mechanics, now known as Schrödinger's equation.
Born on August 12, 1887, in Vienna, Austria, into an intellectually stimulating family with interests in botany and painting.
Developed the famous thought experiment known as 'Schrödinger's cat' in 1935 to illustrate the paradoxes of quantum superposition.
Studied at the University of Vienna, earning a doctorate in physics in 1910 under the supervision of Friedrich Hasenöhrl.
Served as an artillery officer in the Austrian army during World War I before resuming his academic career.
Published a series of groundbreaking papers in 1926 that laid the foundation for wave mechanics, a key component of quantum theory.
Held academic positions at the University of Zurich, University of Berlin, and later at the University of Oxford after fleeing Nazi Germany in 1933.
Wrote the influential book *What Is Life?* in 1944, exploring the physical aspects of the living cell and inspiring future molecular biologists.
Married Annemarie Bertel in 1920, though the relationship was marked by infidelities, including affairs that produced children with other women.
Shared the 1933 Nobel Prize with Paul Dirac, who independently developed a different formulation of quantum mechanics.
Spent time in Dublin, Ireland, from 1940 to 1956 as the Director of the School for Theoretical Physics at the Dublin Institute for Advanced Studies.
Published over 50 scientific papers and several books, covering topics from quantum physics to philosophy and color theory.
Died on January 4, 1961, in Vienna from tuberculosis, leaving behind a legacy that bridges physics, biology, and philosophy.
His name is immortalized in the Schrödinger equation, a fundamental equation in quantum mechanics that describes how quantum systems evolve over time.
Advocated for a unified field theory, attempting to reconcile quantum mechanics with general relativity, though he did not succeed in this endeavor.

summary.fr: Ce scientifique autrichien du début du XXe siècle a révolutionné la physique théorique avec ses travaux sur la mécanique quantique. Il est surtout connu pour son équation d'onde et son célèbre paradoxe du chat, illustrant les mystères de la superposition quantique. Lauréat du prix Nobel de physique en 1933, il a aussi inspiré la biologie moléculaire avec son livre *Qu’est-ce que la vie ?*.
summary.en: This early 20th-century Austrian scientist transformed theoretical physics with his work on quantum mechanics. He is best known for Schrödinger’s equation and the famous 'Schrödinger’s cat' thought experiment, highlighting quantum superposition. Awarded the Nobel Prize in Physics in 1933, he also influenced molecular biology with his book *What Is Life?*.

name: Blaise Pascal
facts: Born on June 19, 1623, in Clermont-Ferrand, France, into a family of a tax collector with an interest in science and mathematics.
Invented one of the first mechanical calculators, the Pascaline, at the age of 19 to help his father with tax calculations.
Formulated Pascal's Law in fluid mechanics, which states that pressure applied to a confined fluid is transmitted undiminished throughout the fluid.
Contributed significantly to the development of probability theory through correspondence with Pierre de Fermat, laying the groundwork for modern statistics.
Published *Traité du triangle arithmétique* in 1654, introducing what is now known as Pascal's Triangle, a fundamental concept in combinatorics.
Conducted experiments on atmospheric pressure and demonstrated that a vacuum could exist, disproving the Aristotelian notion that 'nature abhors a vacuum'.
Invented the syringe and the hydraulic press, applying principles of fluid mechanics to practical devices.
Wrote *Pensées*, a collection of fragments on theology and philosophy, which remains a classic of French literature and Christian apologetics.
Developed the concept of expected value in probability, influencing later economists and decision theorists.
Suffered from chronic ill health, including severe digestive problems and migraines, which limited his ability to work consistently.
Experienced a profound religious conversion in 1654 after a near-fatal carriage accident, leading to a shift in focus toward philosophy and theology.
Collaborated with mathematician Gilles de Roberval to study the cycloid, a curve traced by a point on the rim of a rolling circle.
Died on August 19, 1662, at the age of 39 in Paris, leaving many of his works unfinished or unpublished.
The programming language Pascal, created in 1970, was named in honor of his contributions to mathematics and computing.

summary.fr: Ce scientifique et philosophe français du XVIIe siècle a marqué les mathématiques et la physique. Il a inventé l'une des premières machines à calculer, la Pascaline, et formulé la loi de Pascal en mécanique des fluides. Ses travaux sur les probabilités et le triangle arithmétique restent fondamentaux aujourd'hui.
summary.en: This 17th-century French scientist and philosopher made groundbreaking contributions to mathematics and physics. He invented one of the first mechanical calculators, the Pascaline, and formulated Pascal's Law in fluid mechanics. His work on probability theory and Pascal's Triangle remains foundational in modern science.

name: Lise Meitner
facts: Was born on November 7, 1878, in Vienna, Austria, into a Jewish family.
Became the second woman to earn a doctoral degree in physics from the University of Vienna in 1905.
Collaborated with chemist Otto Hahn for over 30 years, discovering several new isotopes.
Discovered the radiationless transition known as the Auger effect, independently of Pierre Auger.
Fled Nazi Germany in 1938 due to her Jewish heritage, settling in Sweden.
Provided the first theoretical explanation for nuclear fission in 1939, coining the term itself.
Worked with her nephew Otto Frisch to publish the first physical theory of nuclear fission.
Was excluded from the 1944 Nobel Prize in Chemistry awarded to Otto Hahn for nuclear fission, sparking controversy.
Received the Enrico Fermi Award in 1966, becoming the first woman to win it.
Had an element, meitnerium (Mt, atomic number 109), named in her honor in 1997.
Worked at the Nobel Institute in Stockholm and later became a Swedish citizen in 1949.
Declined an offer to work on the Manhattan Project, stating she wanted nothing to do with a bomb.
Published over 120 scientific papers throughout her career, despite facing gender and political barriers.
Died on October 27, 1968, in Cambridge, England, at the age of 89.
Was known for her modesty, often downplaying her own contributions to scientific discoveries.

summary.fr: Cette scientifique autrichienne, née en 1878, a marqué l'histoire de la physique en expliquant pour la première fois le phénomène de la fission nucléaire. Bien qu'elle ait collaboré pendant des décennies avec Otto Hahn, son rôle crucial fut longtemps ignoré, notamment lors de l'attribution du prix Nobel. Un élément chimique porte aujourd'hui son nom en hommage à ses découvertes.
summary.en: This Austrian physicist, born in 1878, made groundbreaking contributions to nuclear physics by explaining nuclear fission for the first time. Despite her long collaboration with Otto Hahn, she was overlooked for the Nobel Prize, though her work was later honored with an element named after her.

name: Ramanujan
facts: Was born on December 22, 1887, in Erode, Madras Presidency, British India (now Tamil Nadu, India).
Displayed extraordinary mathematical skills from a young age, mastering advanced trigonometry by the age of 12.
Failed to complete college due to neglecting non-mathematical subjects, leading to the loss of a scholarship.
Worked as a clerk at the Madras Port Trust while independently pursuing mathematical research.
Wrote to several prominent mathematicians in Cambridge, including G. H. Hardy, sharing his theorems and results in 1913.
Moved to Cambridge in 1914 at Hardy's invitation, despite initial reluctance due to health and cultural concerns.
Collaborated with G. H. Hardy to produce groundbreaking work in number theory, infinite series, and continued fractions.
Developed the Ramanujan theta function and the Ramanujan prime, contributing significantly to mathematical analysis and number theory.
Published numerous papers in prestigious journals, including 37 in a span of four years during his time in England.
Elected a Fellow of the Royal Society in 1918 at the age of 30, one of the youngest in its history.
Became the first Indian to be elected a Fellow of Trinity College, Cambridge, in the same year.
Suffered from poor health throughout his life, exacerbated by the cold English climate and dietary restrictions.
Returned to India in 1919 due to declining health and died on April 26, 1920, at the age of 32.
Left behind notebooks filled with thousands of results, many of which were unproven and later verified by other mathematicians.
The number 1729, known as the Hardy-Ramanujan number, is famous for being the smallest number expressible as the sum of two cubes in two different ways.

summary.fr: Ce mathématicien indien, né en 1887, a marqué l'histoire par son génie précoce et ses découvertes en théorie des nombres. Autodidacte, il a collaboré avec G. H. Hardy à Cambridge, malgré des conditions de vie difficiles. Ses travaux sur les fonctions thêta et les nombres premiers restent fondamentaux en mathématiques.
summary.en: This Indian mathematician, born in 1887, became famous for his extraordinary talent and contributions to number theory. Largely self-taught, he worked with G. H. Hardy in Cambridge, producing groundbreaking results despite health challenges. His work on theta functions and prime numbers remains influential in mathematics.

name: Dmitri Mendeleev
facts: Created the Periodic Table of Elements in 1869, organizing chemical elements by atomic weight and properties.
Predicted the existence and properties of eight elements that had not yet been discovered, including gallium and germanium.
Published over 400 books and articles, contributing significantly to chemistry, physics, and metrology.
Formulated the Periodic Law, which states that elements exhibit periodic properties when arranged by atomic weight.
Worked as a professor at the Saint Petersburg State University from 1867 to 1890.
Developed a version of the periodic table that included gaps for undiscovered elements, earning him widespread recognition.
Wrote the influential textbook *Principles of Chemistry* (1868–1870), which was widely used in education.
Investigated the composition and properties of petroleum, proposing early theories about its origin.
Advocated for the adoption of the metric system in Russia and contributed to the establishment of the first oil refinery in the country.
Received the Davy Medal from the Royal Society in 1882 for his work on the periodic classification of elements.
Was nominated for the Nobel Prize in Chemistry in 1906 but lost by one vote to Henri Moissan.
Had a strong interest in aeronautics and designed a high-altitude balloon for scientific observations.
Was known for his long hair and beard, which he vowed not to cut until Russia adopted a constitution (though he later trimmed it).
Died in 1907 in Saint Petersburg from influenza at the age of 72.
Element 101, mendelevium, was named in his honor in 1955.

summary.fr: Ce scientifique russe du XIXe siècle est célèbre pour avoir créé le tableau périodique des éléments en 1869. Il a organisé les éléments chimiques par poids atomique et a prédit l'existence d'éléments encore inconnus à l'époque. Son travail a révolutionné la chimie et reste une référence mondiale.
summary.en: This 19th-century Russian scientist is best known for creating the Periodic Table of Elements in 1869. He organized chemical elements by atomic weight and predicted the existence of undiscovered elements. His work transformed chemistry and remains foundational in science.

name: Simone de Beauvoir
facts: Born on January 9, 1908, in Paris, France, to a bourgeois family.
Studied philosophy at the Sorbonne, where she met Jean-Paul Sartre, her lifelong partner and intellectual companion.
Graduated in 1929 as the youngest person to ever pass the agrégation in philosophy at the age of 21.
Published her groundbreaking feminist treatise *The Second Sex* in 1949, which analyzed women's oppression and laid the foundation for modern feminism.
Co-founded and wrote for the influential leftist journal *Les Temps Modernes* alongside Sartre and others in 1945.
Wrote the novel *She Came to Stay* in 1943, exploring themes of existentialism, relationships, and the concept of the
Traveled extensively, including to the United States in 1947, where she lectured on existentialism and met American writers like Nelson Algren.
Had a complex and unconventional relationship with Sartre, characterized by open relationships and intellectual collaboration.
Published *The Mandarins* in 1954, a novel about post-World War II intellectuals, which won the Prix Goncourt, France's highest literary honor.
Wrote a four-volume autobiography, including *Memoirs of a Dutiful Daughter* (1958), detailing her early life and intellectual development.
Advocated for women's rights, abortion legalization, and social justice throughout her life, influencing second-wave feminism.
Died of pneumonia on April 14, 1986, in Paris, and was buried alongside Sartre in the Montparnasse Cemetery.
Her work *The Ethics of Ambiguity* (1947) is a key text in existentialist ethics, exploring freedom, responsibility, and moral ambiguity.
Often credited with the famous quote, 'One is not born, but rather becomes, a woman,' from *The Second Sex*.
Remains one of the most influential philosophers of the 20th century, shaping feminist theory and existentialist thought.

summary.fr: Philosophe française du XXe siècle, Simone de Beauvoir est une figure majeure de l'existentialisme et du féminisme. Elle a marqué l'histoire avec son ouvrage *Le Deuxième Sexe*, analysant l'oppression des femmes et fondant les bases du féminisme moderne. Compagne intellectuelle de Jean-Paul Sartre, elle a aussi écrit des romans et des mémoires influents.
summary.en: A 20th-century French philosopher, Simone de Beauvoir was a key figure in existentialism and feminism. She revolutionized feminist thought with *The Second Sex*, which analyzed women's oppression and shaped modern feminism. A lifelong intellectual partner of Jean-Paul Sartre, she also wrote influential novels and memoirs.

name: Jean-Paul Sartre
facts: Born on June 21, 1905, in Paris, France, to a family of intellectuals.
Developed existentialist philosophy, emphasizing individual freedom, choice, and responsibility.
Wrote the influential philosophical work 'Being and Nothingness' in 1943, a cornerstone of existentialist thought.
Co-founded the literary and political review 'Les Temps Modernes' in 1945, which became a leading intellectual journal.
Declined the Nobel Prize in Literature in 1964, citing personal and political reasons.
Had a lifelong open relationship with fellow philosopher and writer Simone de Beauvoir, a pioneer of feminist theory.
Wrote the play 'No Exit' in 1944, which popularized the phrase 'Hell is other people'.
Served as a meteorologist in the French army during World War II and was briefly a prisoner of war.
Was a prominent Marxist and engaged in political activism, including protests against the Algerian War and the Vietnam War.
Published the multi-volume work 'The Roads to Freedom' between 1945 and 1949, exploring themes of existentialism and human freedom.
Delivered a famous lecture in 1945 titled 'Existentialism is a Humanism,' which helped popularize existentialist ideas.
Wrote the biography 'Saint Genet' in 1952, analyzing the life and work of the writer Jean Genet.
Suffered from poor health in later years, including failing eyesight, which limited his ability to write.
Died of pulmonary edema on April 15, 1980, in Paris, and was buried in Montparnasse Cemetery.
Remains one of the most influential philosophers of the 20th century, shaping modern existentialist and phenomenological thought.

summary.fr: Philosophe français du XXe siècle, Jean-Paul Sartre est une figure majeure de l'existentialisme. Il a développé des idées sur la liberté individuelle et la responsabilité, notamment dans son œuvre majeure 'L'Être et le Néant'. Refusant le prix Nobel de littérature en 1964, il a marqué l'histoire par son engagement politique et ses écrits influents.
summary.en: Jean-Paul Sartre was a 20th-century French philosopher and a leading figure in existentialism. He explored themes of individual freedom and responsibility in works like 'Being and Nothingness' and famously declined the Nobel Prize in Literature in 1964. His political activism and intellectual contributions left a lasting impact on philosophy and culture.

name: Friedrich Nietzsche
facts: Was born on October 15, 1844, in Röcken, Prussia (now Germany).
Became a professor of classical philology at the University of Basel at the age of 24.
Resigned from his professorship in 1879 due to poor health, which plagued him for much of his life.
Wrote his first major work, *The Birth of Tragedy*, in 1872, exploring Greek culture and art.
Developed a close but later strained friendship with composer Richard Wagner.
Published *Thus Spoke Zarathustra* between 1883 and 1885, introducing the concept of the Übermensch (Overman).
Coined the phrase 'God is dead' to describe the decline of religion and traditional morality in modern society.
Suffered a mental collapse in 1889 and spent the last decade of his life in a state of incapacity.
Wrote *Beyond Good and Evil* in 1886, critiquing traditional moral values and advocating for a revaluation of all values.
Influenced existentialist thinkers like Jean-Paul Sartre and Albert Camus with his ideas on individualism and meaning.
Died on August 25, 1900, in Weimar, Germany, from pneumonia and a stroke.
His sister, Elisabeth Förster-Nietzsche, edited and published his unpublished notes as *The Will to Power* after his death.
Criticized democracy, nationalism, and anti-Semitism, though his works were later misused by Nazi ideologues.
Advocated for the concept of 'eternal recurrence,' the idea that life repeats infinitely in the same sequence of events.

image.author: Friedrich Hermann Hartmann

summary.fr: Ce philosophe allemand du XIXe siècle a marqué l'histoire par ses idées radicales sur la morale et l'existence. Il est célèbre pour avoir déclaré 'Dieu est mort' et pour son concept du Surhomme. Ses œuvres, comme *Ainsi parlait Zarathoustra*, ont influencé l'existentialisme et la pensée moderne.
summary.en: This 19th-century German philosopher is known for his radical ideas on morality and existence. He famously declared 'God is dead' and introduced the concept of the Übermensch. His works, like *Thus Spoke Zarathustra*, influenced existentialism and modern thought.

name: Immanuel Kant
facts: Born on April 22, 1724, in Königsberg, Prussia (now Kaliningrad, Russia).
Spent nearly his entire life in Königsberg, rarely traveling more than 100 miles from his hometown.
Studied philosophy, mathematics, and physics at the University of Königsberg, where he later became a professor.
Published 'Critique of Pure Reason' in 1781, a foundational work in Western philosophy that explored the limits of human knowledge.
Developed the philosophical concept of the 'categorical imperative,' a central idea in his moral philosophy outlined in 'Groundwork of the Metaphysics of Morals' (1785).
Argued that space and time are not external realities but forms of human intuition in his transcendental idealism.
Wrote 'Critique of Practical Reason' (1788), which focused on ethics and the role of reason in moral decision-making.
Published 'Critique of Judgment' (1790), examining aesthetics and teleology, bridging his earlier works on knowledge and morality.
Proposed the idea of perpetual peace in his 1795 essay 'Toward Perpetual Peace,' influencing modern international relations theory.
Known for his strict daily routine, including a famous afternoon walk so punctual that locals set their clocks by it.
Never married and lived a highly disciplined, almost ascetic lifestyle, dedicating his life to scholarship.
Died on February 12, 1804, in Königsberg, with his last words reportedly being 'Es ist gut' ('It is good').
Influenced countless philosophers, including Hegel, Schopenhauer, and Nietzsche, shaping modern Western thought.
His philosophy marked a turning point in the Enlightenment, synthesizing rationalism and empiricism.

summary.fr: Ce philosophe allemand du XVIIIe siècle a marqué l'histoire par ses travaux sur la connaissance, la morale et la métaphysique. Il est célèbre pour sa théorie du 'impératif catégorique' et son ouvrage majeur, la 'Critique de la raison pure'. Son influence sur la philosophie occidentale reste majeure aujourd'hui.
summary.en: This 18th-century German philosopher shaped modern thought with his work on knowledge, ethics, and metaphysics. He is best known for the 'categorical imperative' and his groundbreaking book, 'Critique of Pure Reason'. His ideas continue to influence philosophy and ethics worldwide.

name: Fyodor Dostoevsky
facts: Born on November 11, 1821, in Moscow, Russia, into a family of modest means.
Studied engineering at the Military Engineering Academy in St. Petersburg but developed a passion for literature.
Published his first novel, 'Poor Folk,' in 1846, which received critical acclaim.
Arrested in 1849 for participating in a literary group discussing banned books critical of Tsarist Russia.
Sentenced to death by firing squad, but the sentence was commuted to four years of hard labor in Siberia at the last moment.
Spent four years in a Siberian prison camp (1850-1854), an experience that deeply influenced his later works.
Wrote 'Notes from the House of the Dead' (1860-1862), a semi-autobiographical novel based on his prison experiences.
Suffered from epilepsy throughout his life, a condition often reflected in his characters.
Published 'Crime and Punishment' in 1866, one of his most famous novels exploring themes of morality and redemption.
Wrote 'The Idiot' (1868-1869), featuring Prince Myshkin, a Christ-like figure navigating a corrupt society.
Authored 'Demons' (1871-1872), a political novel inspired by the rise of revolutionary movements in Russia.
Completed his final novel, 'The Brothers Karamazov' (1880), considered one of the greatest works in world literature.
Struggled with gambling addiction, which led to financial difficulties and inspired the novella 'The Gambler' (1866).
Died on February 9, 1881, in St. Petersburg from a pulmonary hemorrhage linked to emphysema and epilepsy.

image.commons_file: Vasily Perov - Портрет Ф.М.Достоевского - Google Art Project.jpg
image.author: Vasily Perov

summary.fr: Cet écrivain russe du XIXe siècle est l'un des plus grands romanciers de l'histoire. Connu pour ses œuvres profondes comme 'Crime et Châtiment' et 'Les Frères Karamazov', il a exploré la psychologie humaine et les questions morales. Son expérience en prison en Sibérie a marqué à jamais son écriture et sa vision du monde.
summary.en: This 19th-century Russian novelist is one of history’s greatest writers. Famous for masterpieces like 'Crime and Punishment' and 'The Brothers Karamazov,' he delved into human psychology and moral dilemmas. His time in a Siberian prison camp deeply shaped his work and worldview.

name: Franz Kafka
facts: Born on July 3, 1883, in Prague, then part of the Austro-Hungarian Empire, to a middle-class German-speaking Jewish family.
Studied law at the German Charles-Ferdinand University in Prague, earning a doctorate in 1906.
Worked for most of his career at the Workers' Accident Insurance Institute in Prague, a job he held from 1908 until his early retirement in 1922 due to tuberculosis.
Wrote in German, though Czech was also widely spoken in Prague, and his works often explore themes of alienation, bureaucracy, and existential anxiety.

Published only a few short stories during his lifetime, including 'The Metamorphosis' (1915), which begins with the famous line, 'As Gregor Samsa awoke one morning from uneasy dreams he found himself transformed in his bed into a gigantic insect.'
Had a complicated relationship with his father, Hermann Kafka, which deeply influenced his writing, particularly the unfinished letter 'Letter to His Father' (1919).
Engaged multiple times to Felice Bauer, a relationship documented in the collection 'Letters to Felice' (published posthumously), but never married her or anyone else.
Suffered from tuberculosis for much of his adult life, which ultimately led to his death on June 3, 1924, at the age of 40.
Requested in his will that his unpublished manuscripts, including 'The Trial,' 'The Castle,' and 'Amerika,' be burned by his friend Max Brod, who instead published them posthumously.
Wrote 'The Trial' (1925) in 1914–1915, a novel about a man arrested and prosecuted by a remote, inaccessible authority, with the nature of his crime never revealed.

His novella 'The Metamorphosis' is one of the most widely analyzed and discussed works in 20th-century literature, often interpreted as an allegory for mental illness, isolation, or the human condition.
Influenced a wide range of writers, philosophers, and artists, including Albert Camus, Jean-Paul Sartre, Jorge Luis Borges, and Gabriel García Márquez.
The term 'Kafkaesque' was coined to describe situations or works that resemble the nightmarish, bureaucratic, and absurd qualities of his fiction.
Spent time in sanatoriums in Austria and Germany during the last years of his life, seeking treatment for his tuberculosis.
Died in Kierling, near Vienna, and was buried in the New Jewish Cemetery in Prague.

summary.fr: Né en 1883 à Prague, Franz Kafka est un écrivain austro-hongrois de langue allemande, célèbre pour ses œuvres explorant l'absurdité de la bureaucratie et l'angoisse existentielle. Bien qu'il n'ait publié que peu de textes de son vivant, comme *La Métamorphose*, ses romans posthumes, comme *Le Procès*, ont marqué la littérature du XXe siècle.
summary.en: Born in 1883 in Prague, Franz Kafka was an Austro-Hungarian writer who wrote in German, known for his works exploring bureaucracy and existential anxiety. Though he published little during his lifetime, like *The Metamorphosis*, his posthumous novels, such as *The Trial*, became foundational in 20th-century literature.

name: Gabriel Garcia Marquez
facts: Won the Nobel Prize in Literature in 1982 for his novels and short stories, where the fantastic and the realistic are combined in a richly composed world of imagination.
Born on March 6, 1927, in Aracataca, Colombia, a town that inspired the fictional Macondo in his most famous works.
Raised by his maternal grandparents, who deeply influenced his storytelling with their tales of folklore and superstition.
Studied law at the National University of Colombia but abandoned it to pursue journalism and writing.
Worked as a journalist in Colombia and later in Europe, covering significant events like the Cuban Revolution.
Published his first major work, *Leaf Storm*, in 1955, introducing the fictional town of Macondo.
Gained international fame with *One Hundred Years of Solitude* (1967), a landmark novel in magical realism.
Wrote *Love in the Time of Cholera* (1985), a novel exploring enduring love, inspired by his parents' courtship.
Co-founded the *Fundación Nuevo Periodismo Iberoamericano* in 1994 to promote quality journalism in Latin America.
Maintained a close friendship with Cuban leader Fidel Castro, which sparked both admiration and controversy.
Published an autobiography, *Living to Tell the Tale* (2002), covering his early life and literary influences.
His works have been translated into over 30 languages, making him one of the most widely read authors in the world.
Diagnosed with lymphatic cancer in 1999 but continued writing until his health declined in his later years.
Died on April 17, 2014, in Mexico City, where he had lived for many years.

summary.fr: Cet écrivain colombien est célèbre pour avoir créé le réalisme magique, un style où le fantastique se mêle à la réalité. Il a remporté le prix Nobel de littérature en 1982 pour des œuvres comme *Cent ans de solitude*, qui a marqué la littérature mondiale. Ses récits, inspirés par son enfance et la culture latino-américaine, sont traduits dans le monde entier.
summary.en: This Colombian author pioneered magical realism, blending the fantastical with everyday life in his stories. He won the Nobel Prize in Literature in 1982 for works like *One Hundred Years of Solitude*, a landmark in global literature. His tales, rooted in Latin American culture and his upbringing, have been translated into over 30 languages.

name: Jorge Luis Borges
facts: Born on August 24, 1899, in Buenos Aires, Argentina, into a bilingual family that spoke both Spanish and English.
Published his first poem, 'Hymn to the Sea,' at the age of nine, which appeared in a local newspaper.
Moved to Switzerland in 1914 due to his father's failing eyesight and attended the Collège Calvin in Geneva.
Became fluent in multiple languages, including French, German, and Latin, during his time in Europe.
Co-founded the ultraist literary movement in Spain in 1919, which sought to renew poetry through metaphor and elimination of rhyme.
Returned to Argentina in 1921 and began publishing avant-garde poetry and essays in literary journals.
Worked as a librarian at the Miguel Cané Municipal Library in Buenos Aires from 1937 to 1946.
Gradually lost his eyesight due to a hereditary condition, becoming completely blind by the mid-1950s.
Wrote some of his most famous works, including 'Ficciones' (1944) and 'The Aleph' (1949), which blended fantasy, philosophy, and metaphysics.
Served as the director of the National Library of Argentina from 1955 to 1973, despite his blindness.
Shared the first International Publishers' Prize (Prix Formentor) with Samuel Beckett in 1961.
Taught literature at the University of Buenos Aires and gave numerous lectures worldwide despite his visual impairment.
Collaborated with Adolfo Bioy Casares on several works, including the detective fiction parody 'Six Problems for Don Isidro Parodi' (1942).
Received the Jerusalem Prize in 1971 for his contributions to literature and the struggle for freedom.
Died of liver cancer on June 14, 1986, in Geneva, Switzerland, where he had spent his final years.

image.commons_file: Jorge Luis Borges 1951, by Grete Stern (full).jpg
image.author: Grete Stern

summary.fr: Cet écrivain argentin du XXe siècle est célèbre pour ses nouvelles et poèmes mêlant fantastique et philosophie. Aveugle à la fin de sa vie, il a dirigé la Bibliothèque nationale d'Argentine et marqué la littérature par des œuvres comme Ficciones. Son style unique a influencé des générations d'auteurs.
summary.en: This 20th-century Argentine writer is renowned for his short stories and poetry blending fantasy and philosophy. Despite going blind, he led Argentina's National Library and created masterpieces like Ficciones. His innovative work left a lasting impact on world literature.

name: Virginia Woolf
facts: Born on January 25, 1882, in London, England, into an intellectual and well-connected family.
Wrote pioneering modernist novels such as *Mrs Dalloway* (1925) and *To the Lighthouse* (1927).
Developed a stream-of-consciousness narrative style that revolutionized 20th-century literature.
Co-founded the Hogarth Press in 1917 with her husband, Leonard Woolf, publishing works by T.S. Eliot and Katherine Mansfield.
Published the feminist essay *A Room of One's Own* in 1929, arguing for women's financial and creative independence.
Struggled with mental illness throughout her life, experiencing severe bouts of depression and anxiety.
Wrote the experimental novel *Orlando* (1928), a fantastical biography spanning centuries and genders.
Delivered lectures at Cambridge University that later became the basis for *A Room of One's Own*.
Was a central figure in the Bloomsbury Group, an influential collective of writers, artists, and intellectuals.
Published *The Waves* in 1931, a novel composed of soliloquies by six characters, showcasing her innovative prose.
Died by suicide on March 28, 1941, drowning herself in the River Ouse near her home in Sussex.
Left behind a suicide note expressing her fear of losing her sanity and gratitude for her husband's support.
Her posthumously published novel *Between the Acts* (1941) reflects her preoccupation with time and history.
Influenced later writers like Margaret Atwood, Michael Cunningham, and Gabriel García Márquez with her narrative techniques.
Her diaries and letters, published after her death, provide deep insight into her creative process and personal struggles.

image.commons_file: George Charles Beresford - Virginia Woolf in 1902 - Restoration.jpg
image.author: George Charles Beresford / Adam Cuerden

summary.fr: Cette écrivaine britannique du début du XXe siècle a marqué la littérature par son style innovant, comme le flux de conscience. Elle est célèbre pour des œuvres comme *Mrs Dalloway* et *Une chambre à soi*, un essai féministe fondateur. Figure majeure du modernisme, elle a aussi cofondé une maison d’édition influente.
summary.en: This early 20th-century British writer revolutionized literature with her stream-of-consciousness style. Known for novels like *Mrs Dalloway* and the feminist essay *A Room of One’s Own*, she was a key modernist figure. She also co-founded the Hogarth Press, shaping literary publishing.

name: Albert Camus
facts: Born on November 7, 1913, in Mondovi, French Algeria, to a working-class family.
Raised in poverty after his father died in World War I when he was less than a year old.
Studied philosophy at the University of Algiers but was forced to leave due to tuberculosis.
Worked as a journalist for the Alger-Républicain and later for Paris-Soir during the 1930s.
Joined the French Resistance during World War II and edited the underground newspaper Combat.
Published his first novel, *The Stranger* (*L'Étranger*), in 1942, which became a classic of existential literature.
Wrote *The Myth of Sisyphus* (1942), an essay exploring the concept of the absurd in human existence.
Developed the philosophy of absurdism, arguing that life has no inherent meaning but humans seek it anyway.
Won the Nobel Prize in Literature in 1957 for his significant contributions to literature and philosophy.
Published *The Plague* (*La Peste*) in 1947, a novel often interpreted as an allegory for the Nazi occupation of France.
Engaged in a famous public dispute with Jean-Paul Sartre over political and philosophical differences in the 1950s.
Died in a car accident on January 4, 1960, at the age of 46, near Sens, France.
Left behind an unfinished autobiographical novel, *The First Man* (*Le Premier Homme*), published posthumously in 1994.
Known for his humanist views, advocating for justice, freedom, and resistance against oppression.
Often associated with existentialism, though he rejected the label, preferring to be linked with absurdism.

image.commons_file: Albert Camus, gagnant de prix Nobel, portrait en buste, posé au bureau, faisant face à gauche, cigarette de tabagisme.jpg
image.author: Photograph by United Press International

summary.fr: Né en Algérie en 1913, ce philosophe et écrivain français a marqué la littérature par ses œuvres existentialistes comme *L'Étranger* et *Le Mythe de Sisyphe*. Il a reçu le prix Nobel de littérature en 1957 pour ses réflexions sur l'absurdité de la condition humaine. Son engagement dans la Résistance et ses débats avec Sartre ont aussi marqué l'histoire intellectuelle du XXe siècle.
summary.en: Born in Algeria in 1913, this French philosopher and writer became famous for existential works like *The Stranger* and *The Myth of Sisyphus*. He won the Nobel Prize in Literature in 1957 for his exploration of the absurd in human life. His role in the French Resistance and debates with Sartre shaped 20th-century intellectual history.

name: Akira Kurosawa
facts: Born on March 23, 1910, in Tokyo, Japan, to a family with samurai lineage.
Directed 30 films over a 57-year career, spanning from 1943 to 1993.
Won the Golden Lion at the Venice Film Festival in 1951 for *Rashomon*, which also earned an Academy Honorary Award.
Known for pioneering the use of telephoto lenses and multiple cameras to capture action sequences dynamically.
Collaborated frequently with actor Toshiro Mifune, who starred in 16 of his films, including *Seven Samurai* and *Yojimbo*.
Influenced numerous Western filmmakers, including George Lucas, Martin Scorsese, and Francis Ford Coppola.
Directed *Seven Samurai* (1954), which inspired the Hollywood remake *The Magnificent Seven* (1960).
Survived a suicide attempt in 1971 after the commercial failure of *Dodes'ka-den* and a period of depression.
Won the Academy Award for Best Foreign Language Film in 1976 for *Dersu Uzala*, a Soviet-Japanese co-production.
Directed *Ran* (1985), a loose adaptation of Shakespeare’s *King Lear*, which earned him an Oscar nomination for Best Director.
Worked as an assistant director and screenwriter before making his directorial debut with *Sanshiro Sugata* in 1943.
Often incorporated themes of humanism, existentialism, and social critique into his films, such as *Ikiru* (1952).
Received the Legion of Honour from France in 1984 for his contributions to cinema.
His film *Throne of Blood* (1957) is a renowned adaptation of Shakespeare’s *Macbeth*, set in feudal Japan.
Passed away on September 6, 1998, in Tokyo, at the age of 88, leaving behind an unfinished project titled *The Sea is Watching*.

summary.fr: Ce réalisateur japonais du XXe siècle est l'un des plus influents de l'histoire du cinéma. Il a marqué le monde avec des films comme *Les Sept Samouraïs* et *Rashomon*, qui ont inspiré des générations de cinéastes occidentaux. Connu pour son humanisme et ses techniques innovantes, il a remporté de nombreux prix internationaux.
summary.en: This 20th-century Japanese filmmaker is one of the most influential directors in cinema history. He gained global fame with films like *Seven Samurai* and *Rashomon*, which inspired generations of Western directors. Renowned for his humanist themes and innovative techniques, he won multiple international awards.

name: Federico Fellini
facts: Born on January 20, 1920, in Rimini, Italy, into a middle-class family.
Initially worked as a cartoonist and writer for humor magazines before entering the film industry.
Collaborated with Roberto Rossellini on the screenplay for 'Rome, Open City' (1945), a key film in Italian neorealism.
Directed his first solo feature film, 'Variety Lights' (1950), co-directed with Alberto Lattuada.
Gained international acclaim with 'La Strada' (1954), starring Giulietta Masina and Anthony Quinn, which won the Academy Award for Best Foreign Language Film.
Married actress Giulietta Masina in 1943, who became his muse and starred in several of his films.
Known for blending fantasy, dreams, and reality in films like '8½' (1963), a semi-autobiographical masterpiece about a director's creative block.
Won four Academy Awards for Best Foreign Language Film, more than any other director in history at the time.
Directed 'La Dolce Vita' (1960), which introduced the term 'paparazzi' into popular culture.
Received an honorary Oscar in 1993 for lifetime achievement, one of the few directors to receive this honor.
Often worked with composer Nino Rota, whose scores became synonymous with his films' whimsical and nostalgic tone.
Suffered a stroke in 1993 and died on October 31 of the same year, just one day after his 50th wedding anniversary.
The Federico Fellini Airport in Rimini is named in his honor, reflecting his lasting impact on Italian culture.
His films frequently explored themes of memory, nostalgia, and the blurred line between illusion and reality.
Influenced generations of filmmakers, including Woody Allen, David Lynch, and Pedro Almodóvar.

image.author: Walter Albertin, World Telegram staff photographer

summary.fr: Ce réalisateur italien du XXe siècle est célèbre pour ses films oniriques mêlant réalité et fantaisie. Il a marqué le cinéma avec des œuvres comme 'La Dolce Vita' et '8½', remportant quatre Oscars du meilleur film étranger. Son style unique a influencé des générations de cinéastes.
summary.en: This 20th-century Italian filmmaker is renowned for his dreamlike movies blending reality and fantasy. He left a lasting mark on cinema with works like 'La Dolce Vita' and '8½', winning four Academy Awards for Best Foreign Language Film. His distinctive style influenced generations of directors.

name: Ingmar Bergman
facts: Born on July 14, 1918, in Uppsala, Sweden, to a Lutheran minister father and a nurse mother.
Directed over 60 films and documentaries during a career spanning more than six decades.
Wrote most of his film scripts, often exploring themes of existentialism, faith, and human relationships.
Known for his collaborations with cinematographer Sven Nykvist, which produced visually striking and emotionally intense films.
Directed the 1957 film 'The Seventh Seal,' featuring a knight playing chess with Death, a scene that became iconic in cinema history.
Won the Academy Award for Best Foreign Language Film three times: for 'The Virgin Spring' (1960), 'Through a Glass Darkly' (1961), and 'Fanny and Alexander' (1982).
Created the 1966 film 'Persona,' a psychological drama often cited as one of the greatest films ever made.
Worked extensively in theater, serving as the director of the Royal Dramatic Theatre in Stockholm from 1963 to 1966.
Published an autobiography titled 'The Magic Lantern' in 1987, detailing his life and artistic journey.
Suffered from severe depression and was hospitalized in the 1970s, an experience that influenced his later works.
Directed the 1973 miniseries 'Scenes from a Marriage,' which became a cultural phenomenon and was later edited into a feature film.
Received the Irving G. Thalberg Memorial Award at the 1971 Academy Awards for his contributions to cinema.
Spent his later years on the remote Swedish island of Fårö, where he filmed several of his movies, including 'Through a Glass Darkly' and 'Persona.'
Passed away on July 30, 2007, at the age of 89, on the same day as another cinema legend, Michelangelo Antonioni.
Left behind a legacy of influential films that continue to inspire filmmakers and audiences worldwide.

summary.fr: Ce réalisateur suédois du XXe siècle est l'un des plus influents de l'histoire du cinéma. Connu pour ses films profonds explorant la foi, l'angoisse existentielle et les relations humaines, il a marqué le 7e art avec des œuvres comme 'Le Septième Sceau' et 'Persona'. Trois de ses films ont remporté l'Oscar du meilleur film étranger.
summary.en: This 20th-century Swedish filmmaker is one of the most influential directors in cinema history. Renowned for his profound explorations of faith, existential angst, and human relationships, he created masterpieces like 'The Seventh Seal' and 'Persona'. He won the Academy Award for Best Foreign Language Film three times.

name: Andrei Tarkovsky
facts: Born on April 4, 1932, in Zavrazhye, Soviet Union, to poet Arseny Tarkovsky and Maria Vishnyakova.
Graduated from the State Institute of Cinematography (VGIK) in Moscow in 1960, studying under Mikhail Romm.
Directed his first feature film, *Ivan's Childhood*, in 1962, which won the Golden Lion at the Venice Film Festival.
Known for his deeply philosophical and spiritual approach to filmmaking, often exploring themes of memory, time, and human existence.
Created only seven feature films in his career, each widely regarded as a masterpiece of world cinema.
Directed *Andrei Rublev* (1966), a historical epic about the 15th-century Russian icon painter, which was initially banned in the Soviet Union.
Released *Solaris* (1972), a science fiction film based on Stanisław Lem’s novel, often compared to Stanley Kubrick’s *2001: A Space Odyssey*.
Directed *Mirror* (1975), a semi-autobiographical film blending poetry, dreams, and historical footage, considered one of his most personal works.
Exiled from the Soviet Union in 1982 after facing censorship and creative restrictions, eventually settling in Italy and later Sweden.
Directed *Nostalghia* (1983) in Italy, a film about a Russian poet’s longing for his homeland, which won the Grand Prix du Cinéma de Création at Cannes.
Created *The Sacrifice* (1986), his final film, shot in Sweden and exploring themes of faith and apocalypse, which won the Grand Prix at Cannes.
Wrote the book *Sculpting in Time* (1986), a theoretical work on the art of cinema, detailing his philosophy of filmmaking.
Died of lung cancer on December 29, 1986, in Paris, France, at the age of 54.
Posthumously awarded the Lenin Prize in 1990, one of the highest honors in the Soviet Union, for his contributions to cinema.
Influenced generations of filmmakers, including Lars von Trier, Christopher Nolan, and Alejandro González Iñárritu, with his unique visual and narrative style.

summary.fr: Ce réalisateur russe du XXe siècle est célèbre pour ses films poétiques et philosophiques, explorant des thèmes comme le temps et la mémoire. Ses œuvres, comme *Andrei Rublev* ou *Solaris*, sont considérées comme des chefs-d'œuvre du cinéma mondial. Il a marqué l'histoire du 7e art par son approche spirituelle et son style unique, malgré une carrière limitée en nombre de films.
summary.en: This 20th-century Russian filmmaker is renowned for his poetic and philosophical movies, often exploring themes of time and memory. His works, such as *Andrei Rublev* and *Solaris*, are regarded as masterpieces of world cinema. Despite a small filmography, he left a lasting impact on the art of filmmaking with his spiritual approach and distinctive style.

name: Billie Holiday
facts: Born Eleanora Fagan on April 7, 1915, in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania.
Raised in Baltimore, Maryland, and had a difficult childhood marked by poverty and neglect.
Began singing in Harlem nightclubs in the early 1930s, often performing with jazz pianist Teddy Wilson.
Known for her distinctive vocal style, which included a unique phrasing and emotional depth.
Collaborated with legendary musicians such as Lester Young, who gave her the nickname 'Lady Day'.
Recorded the iconic song 'Strange Fruit' in 1939, a powerful protest against lynching and racial violence.
Signed with Columbia Records in 1933 and later with Commodore Records, producing numerous hits.
Released the autobiographical song 'God Bless the Child' in 1941, which became one of her signature tunes.
Faced severe racial discrimination throughout her career, often being forced to enter venues through back doors.
Struggled with drug addiction and legal troubles, including a 1947 arrest for narcotics possession.
Published an autobiography titled 'Lady Sings the Blues' in 1956, detailing her life and career.
Performed a historic concert at Carnegie Hall in 1948, which was later released as a live album.
Received four posthumous Grammy Awards, including a Lifetime Achievement Award in 1987.
Died on July 17, 1959, in New York City due to complications from cirrhosis of the liver.
Influenced countless musicians, including Frank Sinatra, who cited her as a major inspiration.

summary.fr: Cette artiste américaine, née en 1915, est une légende du jazz connue pour sa voix unique et émouvante. Elle a marqué l'histoire avec des chansons comme Strange Fruit, un puissant hymne contre le racisme. Son influence sur la musique du XXe siècle reste inégalée.
summary.en: This American artist, born in 1915, is a jazz legend celebrated for her distinctive and deeply emotional voice. She made history with songs like Strange Fruit, a bold protest against racial violence. Her impact on 20th-century music endures.

name: Nina Simone

attributes.subfield: jazz, blues, soul, classical, gospel, folk, protest music

facts: Born Eunice Kathleen Waymon on February 21, 1933, in Tryon, North Carolina.
Began playing piano at the age of three and gave her first classical recital at age 10.
Was denied admission to the Curtis Institute of Music in Philadelphia, an event she attributed to racial discrimination.
Adopted the stage name 'Nina Simone' in 1954 to hide her music career from her strict religious family.
Gained initial fame with her debut album 'Little Girl Blue' in 1958, featuring the hit 'I Loves You, Porgy'.
Became known for her distinctive voice, blending jazz, blues, classical, and gospel influences.
Wrote and performed civil rights anthems like 'Mississippi Goddam' (1964) and 'To Be Young, Gifted and Black' (1969).
Performed at the Selma to Montgomery marches in 1965, supporting the civil rights movement alongside figures like Martin Luther King Jr.
Lived in Liberia, Switzerland, and France during her self-imposed exile from the United States in the 1970s and 1980s.
Released over 40 albums during her career, spanning genres from classical to protest music.
Was inducted into the Rock and Roll Hall of Fame in 2018, posthumously.
Suffered from bipolar disorder and other mental health struggles, which influenced her later life and career.
Passed away on April 21, 2003, in Carry-le-Rouet, France, at the age of 70.
Her song 'Feeling Good' became widely popular after being covered by artists like Muse and Michael Bublé.
Was the subject of the 2015 Netflix documentary 'What Happened, Miss Simone?', which explored her life and legacy.

summary.fr: Nina Simone était une chanteuse, pianiste et militante américaine, née en 1933. Elle est célèbre pour sa voix unique et son mélange de jazz, blues et musique classique. Engagée dans le mouvement des droits civiques, elle a marqué l'histoire avec des chansons comme *Mississippi Goddam*.
summary.en: Nina Simone was an American singer, pianist, and civil rights activist born in 1933. Known for her powerful voice and fusion of jazz, blues, and classical music, she became a symbol of protest with songs like *Mississippi Goddam*. Her work remains influential in music and social justice.

name: Miles Davis
facts: Born on May 26, 1926, in Alton, Illinois, and raised in East St. Louis, Illinois.
Began playing the trumpet at the age of 13, receiving his first instrument from his father.
Moved to New York City in 1944 to attend the Juilliard School but soon dropped out to pursue jazz full-time.
Played alongside jazz legends like Charlie Parker and Dizzy Gillespie in the mid-1940s bebop scene.
Released the groundbreaking album *Birth of the Cool* in 1957, which introduced the
Pioneered modal jazz with the 1959 album *Kind of Blue*, which remains the best-selling jazz album of all time.
Collaborated with arranger Gil Evans on several albums, including *Porgy and Bess* (1958) and *Sketches of Spain* (1960).
Formed his first great quintet in the mid-1950s, featuring John Coltrane, Red Garland, Paul Chambers, and Philly Joe Jones.
Struggled with drug addiction in the early 1950s but overcame it by the end of the decade.
Innovated jazz fusion with the 1970 album *Bitches Brew*, blending jazz with rock and electronic music.
Won eight Grammy Awards during his career, including a Lifetime Achievement Award in 1990.
Known for his minimalist, introspective playing style and use of a Harmon mute to create a signature sound.
Inducted into the Rock and Roll Hall of Fame in 2006 for his contributions to jazz and fusion.
Suffered from health issues in his later years, including diabetes and respiratory problems, leading to his death on September 28, 1991.
Often wore dark sunglasses on stage, contributing to his enigmatic persona and the nickname 'Prince of Darkness'.

summary.fr: Ce musicien américain a révolutionné le jazz au XXe siècle. Trompettiste de génie, il a marqué l'histoire avec des albums comme *Kind of Blue*, le disque de jazz le plus vendu au monde. Pionnier du jazz modal et du jazz fusion, il a influencé des générations de musiciens.
summary.en: This American musician transformed jazz in the 20th century. A master trumpeter, he made history with albums like *Kind of Blue*, the best-selling jazz record ever. A pioneer of modal jazz and jazz fusion, he inspired generations of artists.

name: John Coltrane
facts: Born on September 23, 1926, in Hamlet, North Carolina, and raised in High Point, North Carolina.
Began playing the clarinet and alto saxophone in high school before switching to the tenor saxophone in his late teens.
Served in the U.S. Navy from 1945 to 1946, where he played in a Navy band.
Joined Dizzy Gillespie’s big band in 1949 and later played with Johnny Hodges and Earl Bostic in the early 1950s.
Struggled with heroin addiction in the early 1950s, which impacted his career until overcoming it in 1957.
Played in Miles Davis’s first great quintet from 1955 to 1957, contributing to albums like *Round About Midnight* and *Milestones*.
Released the groundbreaking album *Giant Steps* in 1960, featuring complex harmonic progressions now known as "Coltrane changes."
Formed his classic quartet in 1960 with pianist McCoy Tyner, bassist Jimmy Garrison, and drummer Elvin Jones.
Composed and recorded *A Love Supreme* in 1964, a spiritual jazz masterpiece widely regarded as one of the greatest albums of all time.
Pioneered avant-garde and free jazz in the mid-1960s, collaborating with artists like Pharoah Sanders and Alice Coltrane.
Received a posthumous Pulitzer Prize Special Citation in 2007 for his contributions to jazz and American music.
Died of liver cancer on July 17, 1967, at the age of 40 in Huntington, New York.
Influenced countless musicians across genres, including rock, classical, and hip-hop, with his innovative approach to improvisation.
The Church of St. John Coltrane in San Francisco, founded in 1971, venerates his music as a spiritual experience.
Inducted into the North Carolina Music Hall of Fame in 2009 and the DownBeat Jazz Hall of Fame in 1965.

summary.fr: Ce musicien américain, né en 1926, est une légende du jazz. Il a révolutionné le saxophone ténor et marqué l'histoire avec des albums comme *Giant Steps* et *A Love Supreme*. Son influence sur le jazz moderne reste immense, malgré sa disparition précoce en 1967.
summary.en: This American musician, born in 1926, was a jazz legend who transformed the tenor saxophone. He created groundbreaking works like *Giant Steps* and *A Love Supreme*, shaping modern jazz before his early death in 1967.

name: Caravaggio
facts: Born on September 29, 1571, in Milan, Italy, to a modest family.
Revolutionized European art with his dramatic use of chiaroscuro, a technique involving strong contrasts between light and dark.
Moved to Rome in 1592, where he initially worked in the studio of Giuseppe Cesari, painting flowers and fruit.
Gained fame with his first major public commission, the *Contarelli Chapel* paintings (1599–1600), including *The Calling of Saint Matthew*.
Known for painting directly from live models, often using ordinary people, including prostitutes, as subjects for religious figures.
Fled Rome in 1606 after killing a man, Ranuccio Tomassoni, in a duel, leading to a death sentence by the Pope.
Spent his final years as a fugitive, traveling to Naples, Malta, and Sicily while continuing to paint masterpieces like *The Beheading of Saint John the Baptist*.
Received a pardon from the Pope in 1610 but died under mysterious circumstances on July 18, 1610, while traveling back to Rome.
Created some of the most iconic works of the Baroque period, including *Judith Beheading Holofernes* and *David with the Head of Goliath*.
