Influenced countless artists, including Rembrandt and Rubens, through his realistic and emotionally charged style.
Often depicted violent or dramatic scenes, reflecting his tumultuous personal life and legal troubles.
His work *The Supper at Emmaus* (1601) is celebrated for its lifelike depiction of human emotion and masterful use of perspective.
Spent time in Malta as a knight of the Order of St. John, where he painted *The Beheading of Saint John the Baptist*, his largest work.
Buried in an unmarked grave, with the exact location of his remains remaining unknown for centuries.

image.author: Ottavio Leoni

summary.fr: Ce peintre italien du XVIe siècle a révolutionné l'art européen avec son usage dramatique du clair-obscur. Connu pour ses scènes religieuses réalistes et ses modèles issus du peuple, il a marqué la période baroque malgré une vie tumultueuse et une fin mystérieuse.
summary.en: This 16th-century Italian painter transformed European art with his dramatic use of chiaroscuro. Famous for his realistic religious scenes and ordinary models, he shaped the Baroque period despite a turbulent life and mysterious death.

name: Gustav Klimt
facts: Born on July 14, 1862, in Baumgarten, near Vienna, Austria-Hungary.
Co-founded the Vienna Secession movement in 1897, promoting unconventional young artists.
Known for his distinctive use of gold leaf in paintings, inspired by Byzantine mosaics.
Created the famous painting *The Kiss* between 1907 and 1908, which became an iconic work of the Art Nouveau era.
Studied at the Vienna School of Arts and Crafts (Kunstgewerbeschule) from 1876 to 1883.
Designed the ceiling paintings for the University of Vienna's Great Hall, which were later destroyed during World War II.
His *Portrait of Adele Bloch-Bauer I* (1907) sold for $135 million in 2006, setting a record for the highest-priced painting at the time.
Often depicted women in his works, exploring themes of sensuality, love, and death.
Worked closely with his brother Ernst Klimt, who was also a painter, until Ernst's death in 1892.
Received the Golden Order of Merit from Emperor Franz Josef I of Austria in 1888.
Suffered a stroke in 1918 that left him partially paralyzed and led to his death on February 6 of the same year.
His later works, such as *Death and Life* (1910), reflected a shift toward more symbolic and allegorical themes.
Never married but had a long-term relationship with fashion designer Emilie Flöge, who was a frequent subject in his portraits.
Influenced by Japanese art and its methods, which is evident in his use of flat, decorative patterns.
The Klimt Villa in Vienna, where he lived and worked, is now a museum dedicated to his life and art.

image.author: Josef Anton Trčka

summary.fr: Peintre autrichien né en 1862, Gustav Klimt est une figure majeure de l'Art Nouveau. Il est célèbre pour ses œuvres dorées comme *Le Baiser* et son rôle dans le mouvement de la Sécession viennoise. Ses tableaux, souvent centrés sur la féminité et la symbolique, ont marqué l'art européen du début du XXe siècle.
summary.en: Austrian painter Gustav Klimt, born in 1862, was a leading figure in the Art Nouveau movement. He is best known for his gold-leaf masterpieces like *The Kiss* and co-founding the Vienna Secession. His works, often exploring themes of love and mortality, defined European art in the early 1900s.

name: Rene Magritte
facts: Born on November 21, 1898, in Lessines, Belgium, into a middle-class family.
Began drawing lessons at the age of 12 and later studied at the Académie Royale des Beaux-Arts in Brussels from 1916 to 1918.
Worked as a draughtsman in a wallpaper factory and designed posters and advertisements to support himself early in his career.
Married Georgette Berger in 1922, who became his muse and appeared in several of his paintings.
Moved to Paris in 1927, where he joined the Surrealist movement and befriended artists like André Breton and Salvador Dalí.
Created one of his most famous works, *The Treachery of Images* (1929), featuring a pipe with the text 'Ceci n'est pas une pipe' ('This is not a pipe').
Returned to Brussels in 1930 due to financial difficulties and remained there for most of his life.
Developed a distinctive style characterized by juxtaposition of ordinary objects in unusual contexts, challenging observers' perceptions of reality.
Painted *The Son of Man* (1964), a self-portrait featuring a man in a bowler hat with an apple obscuring his face, becoming one of his most iconic images.
Produced *Golconda* (1953), depicting men in bowler hats raining down from the sky, blending mundane and surreal elements.
Designed the artwork for the cover of *The Beatles'* 1967 album *Magical Mystery Tour*, though the band ultimately chose a different design.
Died of pancreatic cancer on August 15, 1967, in Brussels, at the age of 68.
Influenced pop art, conceptual art, and advertising, with his works inspiring artists like Andy Warhol and Jasper Johns.
The Magritte Museum in Brussels, opened in 2009, houses the world's largest collection of his works, with over 200 paintings, drawings, and sculptures.

summary.fr: Ce peintre belge du XXe siècle est célèbre pour ses œuvres surréalistes qui jouent avec la réalité et l'illusion. Il a marqué l'art en représentant des objets ordinaires dans des contextes insolites, comme dans *La Trahison des images* où une pipe est accompagnée du texte 'Ceci n'est pas une pipe'. Son style unique a influencé l'art moderne et la culture populaire.
summary.en: This 20th-century Belgian painter is renowned for his surrealist works that challenge perceptions of reality. He became famous for depicting ordinary objects in unusual contexts, such as *The Treachery of Images*, which features a pipe with the text 'This is not a pipe'. His distinctive style left a lasting impact on modern art and popular culture.

name: Yayoi Kusama

attributes.subfield: contemporary art, painting, sculpture, installation, performance

facts: Born on March 22, 1929, in Matsumoto, Nagano Prefecture, Japan, into a family of seedling merchants.
Began creating art at a young age as a way to cope with hallucinations and mental illness, which have influenced her work throughout her career.
Moved to New York City in 1957, where she became a central figure in the avant-garde art scene during the 1960s.
Gained fame for her innovative use of polka dots, which became a signature motif in her paintings, sculptures, and installations.
Created the iconic *Infinity Mirror Rooms*, immersive installations that use mirrors and lights to create the illusion of endless space.
Organized provocative and politically charged happenings in the 1960s, often involving nudity and challenging societal norms.
Collaborated with artists like Andy Warhol, Claes Oldenburg, and Joseph Cornell during her time in New York.
Returned to Japan in 1973 due to declining health and later voluntarily admitted herself to a psychiatric hospital in Tokyo, where she continues to live and work.
Published an autobiography in 2002 titled *Infinity Net*, detailing her life, struggles, and artistic journey.
Represented Japan at the 1993 Venice Biennale, where she exhibited a mirrored room filled with pumpkin sculptures, another recurring motif in her work.
Opened the Yayoi Kusama Museum in Tokyo in 2017, dedicated to showcasing her art and legacy.
Holds the record for the highest auction price for a work by a living female artist, with *White No. 28* (1960) selling for $7.1 million in 2014.
Continues to produce new work well into her 90s, maintaining a prolific output despite her age and health challenges.
Her work explores themes of infinity, obsession, and the interconnectedness of all things, often reflecting her personal struggles with mental illness.
Received numerous awards, including the Order of the Rising Sun (2006) and the Praemium Imperiale (2006) for her contributions to the arts.

image.commons_file: Yayoi Kusama wax model at Louis Vitton.jpg
image.author: Garry Knight

summary.fr: Cette artiste japonaise contemporaine, née en 1929, est célèbre pour ses œuvres audacieuses utilisant des pois et des miroirs. Ses installations immersives comme les *Infinity Mirror Rooms* ont marqué l’art du XXe siècle. Elle a aussi été une figure majeure de l’avant-garde new-yorkaise dans les années 1960.
summary.en: This Japanese contemporary artist, born in 1929, is renowned for her bold use of polka dots and mirrors in her work. Her immersive *Infinity Mirror Rooms* have redefined modern art. She was a key figure in New York’s 1960s avant-garde scene.

name: Roald Amundsen
facts: Born on July 16, 1872, in Borge, Norway, into a family of shipowners and captains.
Originally studied medicine but abandoned it at age 21 to pursue a life at sea following his mother's death.
Served as first mate on the Belgian Antarctic Expedition (1897–1899), the first to overwinter in Antarctica.
Led the first expedition to traverse the Northwest Passage (1903–1906) aboard the ship Gjøa.
Secretly changed his plan from drifting to the North Pole to heading for the South Pole after learning of Cook and Peary's claims to have reached the North Pole.
Became the first person to reach the South Pole on December 14, 1911, beating Robert Falcon Scott's British expedition by 34 days.
Used sled dogs and skis effectively in polar exploration, a key factor in his success at the South Pole.
Led the first expedition to fly over the North Pole in the airship Norge in 1926, alongside Umberto Nobile and Lincoln Ellsworth.
Disappeared in June 1928 while flying on a rescue mission to save Nobile's crashed airship Italia in the Arctic.
His body was never recovered, but wreckage from his plane was found near Bear Island in 1933.
Wrote several books, including 'The South Pole' (1912) and 'My Life as an Explorer' (1927), detailing his expeditions.
Known for his meticulous planning, adaptability, and respect for indigenous peoples' knowledge, such as learning from the Inuit during the Northwest Passage expedition.
Awarded numerous honors, including the Royal Geographical Society's Patron's Medal and the Congressional Gold Medal posthumously.
The Amundsen-Scott South Pole Station, established in 1956, is named in honor of him and his rival Robert Falcon Scott.

summary.fr: Cet explorateur norvégien est célèbre pour avoir été le premier à atteindre le pôle Sud en 1911, devançant de peu l'expédition britannique de Scott. Spécialiste des régions polaires, il a aussi traversé le passage du Nord-Ouest et survolé le pôle Nord en dirigeable. Disparu lors d'une mission de sauvetage en Arctique en 1928, il reste une figure majeure de l'exploration du début du XXe siècle.
summary.en: This Norwegian explorer became the first person to reach the South Pole in 1911, beating Robert Falcon Scott's British team. A pioneer in polar exploration, he also navigated the Northwest Passage and flew over the North Pole in an airship. He vanished during a 1928 Arctic rescue mission, leaving behind a legacy as one of history's greatest explorers.

name: Yuri Gagarin
facts: Became the first human to journey into outer space on April 12, 1961, aboard the Vostok 1 spacecraft.
Completed one orbit around the Earth in 108 minutes during the historic Vostok 1 mission.
Uttered the famous phrase 'Poyekhali!' ('Let's go!') at the moment of launch.
Was selected from over 3,000 candidates to be the first cosmonaut in the Soviet space program.
Served as a Soviet Air Forces pilot before joining the cosmonaut corps in 1960.
Received the title of Hero of the Soviet Union, the nation's highest honor, after the Vostok 1 mission.
The United Nations declared April 12 as the International Day of Human Space Flight in his honor.
Trained rigorously for over a year, including simulations of weightlessness and isolation.
Ejected from the Vostok 1 capsule at an altitude of 7 km and landed separately by parachute.
Wrote a book titled 'Road to the Stars' detailing his experiences and thoughts on space exploration.
Died in a MiG-15 training jet crash on March 27, 1968, at the age of 34.
Posthumously awarded the Order of Lenin and numerous other honors worldwide.
His ashes are interred in the Kremlin Wall Necropolis in Moscow.
A crater on the far side of the Moon and an asteroid (1772 Gagarin) are named after him.
Remains a global symbol of courage and the pioneering spirit of space exploration.

summary.fr: Ce pilote soviétique est devenu le premier humain à voyager dans l'espace le 12 avril 1961 à bord de Vostok 1. Il a accompli une orbite autour de la Terre en 108 minutes, marquant un tournant dans l'exploration spatiale. Héros de l'Union soviétique, il a inspiré des générations de cosmonautes et d'astronautes.
summary.en: This Soviet pilot became the first human to travel into space on April 12, 1961, aboard Vostok 1. He completed one orbit around Earth in 108 minutes, a landmark achievement in space exploration. A Hero of the Soviet Union, he inspired generations of cosmonauts and astronauts.

name: Valentina Tereshkova
facts: Became the first woman to fly in space on June 16, 1963, aboard the Vostok 6 spacecraft.
Completed 48 orbits of Earth during her three-day mission in space.
Used the call sign 'Chaika' (Seagull) during her spaceflight.
Was selected from over 400 applicants to join the Soviet space program's female cosmonaut corps.
Trained rigorously for 18 months before her historic spaceflight, including weightless flights and isolation tests.
Received the title 'Hero of the Soviet Union,' the highest honor in the USSR, for her space mission.
Served as a prominent spokesperson for the Soviet Union, promoting its space achievements internationally.
Earned a doctorate in engineering from the Zhukovsky Air Force Academy in 1977.
Held various political positions, including serving as a member of the Supreme Soviet and the Presidium.
Was awarded the United Nations Gold Medal of Peace for her contributions to space exploration and international cooperation.
Married fellow cosmonaut Andriyan Nikolayev in 1963, though they later divorced in 1982.
Had a daughter, Elena Andrianovna Nikolaeva-Tereshkova, who became the first child born to parents who had both been in space.
Remains the only woman to have completed a solo space mission, as all subsequent female astronauts flew as part of crews.
Was honored by having a crater on the far side of the Moon named after her (Tereshkova crater).
Continues to be an active public figure, advocating for space exploration and women's rights in Russia.

summary.fr: Cette exploratrice russe est devenue la première femme à voyager dans l'espace en 1963 à bord de Vostok 6. Elle a effectué 48 orbites autour de la Terre lors de sa mission historique. Figure emblématique de la conquête spatiale soviétique, elle a marqué l'histoire par son courage et ses contributions scientifiques.
summary.en: This Russian explorer became the first woman to travel to space in 1963 aboard Vostok 6. She completed 48 orbits of Earth during her groundbreaking mission. A pioneering figure in Soviet space exploration, she is celebrated for her bravery and scientific achievements.

name: Ayrton Senna
facts: Won three Formula One World Championships in 1988, 1990, and 1991 driving for McLaren.
Held the record for most pole positions (65) at the time of retirement, a record later surpassed by Michael Schumacher and Lewis Hamilton.
Won the prestigious Monaco Grand Prix six times, a record that stood for over a decade.
Known for his exceptional wet-weather driving skills, earning the nickname 'The Rainmaster'.
Died in a crash during the 1994 San Marino Grand Prix at the age of 34, sending shockwaves through the sports world.
Began karting at the age of 13 and won the South American Kart Championship in 1977.
Made his Formula One debut with Toleman in 1984, famously finishing second in a rain-affected Monaco Grand Prix.
Joined McLaren in 1988 and formed a legendary rivalry with teammate Alain Prost, one of the most intense in F1 history.
Founded the Instituto Ayrton Senna in 1994 to help underprivileged children in Brazil, continuing his philanthropic work posthumously.
Set the fastest lap at the 1993 European Grand Prix at Donington Park, considered one of the greatest laps in F1 history.
Was known for his intense focus and dedication, often spending hours analyzing race data and car performance.
Had a deep spiritual side and often spoke about the presence of a higher power during races, especially in dangerous conditions.
Posthumously inducted into the International Motorsports Hall of Fame in 2000.
The Brazilian Grand Prix circuit in São Paulo was renamed Autódromo José Carlos Pace to Autódromo Ayrton Senna in his honor.
His funeral in Brazil was attended by an estimated three million people, reflecting his status as a national hero.

summary.fr: Ce pilote brésilien de Formule 1 a marqué l'histoire en remportant trois titres mondiaux dans les années 1980 et 1990. Surnommé 'Le Maître de la Pluie' pour son talent en conditions humides, il détient aussi le record de six victoires au Grand Prix de Monaco. Sa carrière tragique s'est terminée en 1994 lors d'un accident, laissant un héritage durable dans le sport automobile.
summary.en: This Brazilian Formula 1 driver made history by winning three World Championships in the 1980s and 1990s. Known as 'The Rainmaster' for his wet-weather skills, he also holds the record for six Monaco Grand Prix wins. His career ended tragically in 1994, but his legacy in motorsport remains unmatched.

name: Nadia Comaneci
facts: Became the first gymnast to be awarded a perfect score of 10.0 at the Olympic Games in 1976 at the age of 14.
Won three gold medals, one silver, and one bronze at the 1976 Montreal Olympics in gymnastics.
Secured two gold medals and two silver medals at the 1980 Moscow Olympics.
Scored a total of seven perfect 10s during the 1976 Montreal Olympics.
Trained under the renowned coaches Béla and Márta Károlyi in Romania.
Won five gold medals at the 1975 European Championships in Skien, Norway.
Defected from Romania to the United States in 1989 with the help of a group of Romanians and Hungarians.
Married American gymnast Bart Conner in 1996, with whom she has a son named Dylan Paul Conner, born in 2006.
Co-owns the Bart Conner Gymnastics Academy in Oklahoma with her husband.
Published an autobiography titled 'Letters to a Young Gymnast' in 2003.
Served as the honorary president of the Romanian Gymnastics Federation and the honorary president of the Romanian Olympic Committee.
Was awarded the Olympic Order, the highest award of the Olympic Movement, in 1984 and 2004.
Inducted into the International Gymnastics Hall of Fame in 1993.
Appeared in the 1984 film 'Nadia' based on her life, though she criticized its accuracy.
Established the Nadia Comaneci Children's Clinic in Bucharest to provide medical care for Romanian children.

summary.fr: Cette sportive roumaine est une légende de la gymnastique. Elle a marqué l'histoire en obtenant la première note parfaite de 10 aux Jeux Olympiques de 1976 à seulement 14 ans. Avec sept notes parfaites et cinq médailles à Montréal, elle reste une icône du sport mondial.
summary.en: This Romanian athlete is a gymnastics legend. She made history by scoring the first perfect 10 at the 1976 Olympic Games at just 14 years old. With seven perfect scores and five medals in Montreal, she remains a global sports icon.

name: Jesse Owens
facts: Won four gold medals in the 1936 Berlin Olympics, defying Adolf Hitler's Aryan supremacy ideology.
Set three world records and tied another in less than an hour at the 1935 Big Ten track meet in Ann Arbor, Michigan.
Became the first American to win four track and field gold medals in a single Olympiad.
Competed for Ohio State University, where he was known as the 'Buckeye Bullet' due to his speed.
Overcame childhood health issues, including chronic bronchial congestion and pneumonia, to become a world-class athlete.
Won the 100 meters, 200 meters, long jump, and 4x100 meter relay events at the 1936 Olympics.
Set a long jump world record of 8.13 meters (26 feet 8 inches) that stood for 25 years.
Faced racial discrimination in the United States despite his Olympic success, including being denied opportunities in sports marketing.
Worked as a playground director and later as a motivational speaker after his athletic career.
Received the Presidential Medal of Freedom from President Gerald Ford in 1976.
Was posthumously awarded the Congressional Gold Medal by President George H.W. Bush in 1990.
Had a street, a school, and a sports complex named in his honor in various parts of the United States.
Published an autobiography titled 'The Jesse Owens Story' in 1970.
Died of lung cancer on March 31, 1980, at the age of 66.

summary.fr: Cet athlète américain a marqué l'histoire en remportant quatre médailles d'or aux Jeux Olympiques de 1936 à Berlin, défiant ainsi l'idéologie raciste d'Adolf Hitler. Spécialiste de la course et du saut en longueur, il a battu plusieurs records du monde dans les années 1930 et est devenu un symbole de résistance et d'excellence sportive malgré les discriminations raciales aux États-Unis.
summary.en: This American track and field athlete made history by winning four gold medals at the 1936 Berlin Olympics, challenging Adolf Hitler’s racist ideology. A sprint and long jump legend, he set multiple world records in the 1930s and became a symbol of perseverance, overcoming racial discrimination in the U.S. despite his global success.

name: Bjorn Borg
facts: Won 11 Grand Slam singles titles between 1974 and 1981, including five consecutive Wimbledon titles from 1976 to 1980.
Became the youngest male player to win the French Open at age 18 in 1974.
Holds the record for the youngest winner of the Italian Open at age 17 in 1974.
Won three consecutive French Open and Wimbledon doubles in 1974 with partner Guillermo Vilas.
Known for his calm demeanor on court, earning the nickname 'Ice Borg'.
Retired from professional tennis at the age of 26 in 1983, making a brief and unsuccessful comeback in the early 1990s.
Won 64 career singles titles, including 16 in a single season (1979).
First player to earn over $1 million in prize money in a single season (1979).
Inducted into the International Tennis Hall of Fame in 1987.
Won the BBC Overseas Sports Personality of the Year award in 1979.
Known for his distinctive two-handed backhand and topspin-heavy playing style.
Had a famous rivalry with John McEnroe, including the epic 1980 Wimbledon final.
Wore Fila tennis apparel, helping to popularize the brand in the 1970s.
Authored an autobiography titled 'Borg: My Life and Game' in 1980.
Founded the Bjorn Borg fashion brand in 2006, focusing on underwear and sportswear.

image.author: Michaël Bemelmans

summary.fr: Ce sportif suédois est une légende du tennis des années 1970. Il a remporté 11 titres du Grand Chelem, dont cinq Wimbledon consécutifs, et détient le record du plus jeune vainqueur de Roland-Garros à 18 ans. Surnommé 'Ice Borg' pour son calme, il a marqué l’histoire par son jeu puissant et son retrait précoce à 26 ans.
summary.en: This Swedish athlete is a tennis legend from the 1970s. He won 11 Grand Slam titles, including five consecutive Wimbledons, and holds the record as the youngest French Open champion at 18. Nicknamed 'Ice Borg' for his composure, he revolutionized the game with his powerful style before retiring at just 26.

name: Sun Tzu
facts: Authored 'The Art of War', one of the most influential military strategy texts in history, likely written during the 5th century BCE.
Served as a general and strategist under King Helü of Wu during the Spring and Autumn period of ancient China.
Credited with leading the Wu forces to victory against the larger Chu state at the Battle of Boju in 506 BCE.
Advocated for the importance of deception, intelligence gathering, and psychological warfare in military campaigns.
Emphasized the principle of 'winning without fighting' by outmaneuvering opponents through superior strategy.
His work 'The Art of War' consists of 13 chapters, each addressing a different aspect of warfare and leadership.
Influenced not only military leaders but also business executives, politicians, and legal strategists worldwide.
The text was first translated into French in 1772 by the Jesuit Jean Joseph Marie Amiot, introducing it to the Western world.
Some historians debate whether he was a real historical figure or a composite of multiple military strategists.
His teachings stress the importance of adaptability, terrain analysis, and understanding both one's own and the enemy's strengths and weaknesses.
Quotes from 'The Art of War' include 'Know yourself and know your enemy, and in a hundred battles you will never be in peril.'
The book has been studied by military academies globally, including West Point in the United States.
His strategies have been applied to fields beyond warfare, such as corporate competition, sports, and cybersecurity.
The exact dates of birth and death remain uncertain, with estimates placing his life between the 6th and 5th centuries BCE.
Legends claim he was tested by King Helü, who asked him to train a harem of concubines into soldiers as proof of his skills.

summary.fr: Stratège militaire chinois de l'Antiquité, Sun Tzu est célèbre pour avoir écrit 'L'Art de la Guerre', un traité influent sur la stratégie et la tactique. Ses enseignements, datant probablement du Ve siècle avant J.-C., sont encore étudiés aujourd'hui dans les domaines militaire, politique et économique pour leur sagesse intemporelle.
summary.en: An ancient Chinese military strategist, Sun Tzu is best known for authoring 'The Art of War', a highly influential text on strategy and tactics. His teachings, likely from the 5th century BCE, remain widely studied in military, political, and business fields for their timeless wisdom.

name: Hypatia
facts: Was a Hellenistic Neoplatonist philosopher, astronomer, and mathematician who lived in Alexandria, Egypt, then part of the Eastern Roman Empire.
Born around the year 360 AD, though the exact date is uncertain.
Was the daughter of Theon of Alexandria, a well-known mathematician and astronomer who was her first teacher.
Became the head of the Platonist school at Alexandria around 400 AD, where she taught philosophy and astronomy.
Made significant contributions to the fields of mathematics and astronomy, including work on conic sections and the design of astrolabes.
Edited the works of notable mathematicians such as Apollonius of Perga and Diophantus, which are still studied today.
Wrote commentaries on important mathematical texts, including Ptolemy's Almagest and Diophantus's Arithmetica.
Was known for her eloquence, modesty, and beauty, as well as her intellectual prowess.
Advocated for a rational and scientific approach to understanding the universe, aligning with Neoplatonist philosophy.
Taught a diverse group of students, including pagans, Christians, and foreigners, emphasizing tolerance and intellectual curiosity.
Invented or improved several scientific instruments, including a device for distilling water and a plane astrolabe.
Was brutally murdered in March 415 AD by a mob of Christian zealots during a period of religious and political unrest in Alexandria.
Her death is often seen as a symbol of the end of the classical era of Greek mathematics and science.
Inspired numerous works of art, literature, and film, including the 2009 movie 'Agora' directed by Alejandro Amenábar.
The lunar crater Hypatia and the asteroid 238 Hypatia are named in her honor.

summary.fr: Cette philosophe et mathématicienne égyptienne a vécu à Alexandrie au IVe siècle. Elle a dirigé l'école platonicienne et contribué aux mathématiques et à l'astronomie, notamment en améliorant des instruments scientifiques. Son assassinat tragique marque un tournant dans l'histoire des sciences et de la tolérance intellectuelle.
summary.en: This Egyptian mathematician and philosopher lived in Alexandria during the 4th century. She led the Platonist school and advanced mathematics and astronomy, including improving scientific instruments. Her brutal murder in 415 AD remains a dark moment in the history of science and intellectual freedom.

name: Boudicca
facts: Led a major uprising against the occupying forces of the Roman Empire in AD 60 or 61.
Became queen of the Iceni tribe in what is now East Anglia, England, after her husband Prasutagus's death.
Roman administrators ignored Prasutagus's will, which had left his kingdom jointly to his daughters and the Roman emperor, leading to her rebellion.
Famously flogged by the Romans and witnessed her daughters being assaulted, which fueled her desire for vengeance.
United several British tribes, including the Trinovantes, in her revolt against Roman rule.
Destroyed the Roman settlements of Camulodunum (Colchester), Londinium (London), and Verulamium (St Albans).
Estimated to have commanded an army of over 100,000 warriors during her rebellion.
Defeated a Roman legion, the IX Hispana, sent to quell her uprising.
Ultimately defeated by Roman governor Gaius Suetonius Paulinus in the Battle of Watling Street.
Chose to poison herself rather than be captured by the Romans after her defeat.
Her rebellion is considered one of the most significant uprisings against Roman rule in Britain.
Commemorated with a statue near the Houses of Parliament in London, depicting her on a chariot with her daughters.
Inspired numerous works of art, literature, and drama, including poems by Alfred, Lord Tennyson.
Her name is derived from the Celtic word 'bouda,' meaning victory.
Remains a symbol of resistance and female leadership in British history.

summary.fr: Reine celte de la tribu des Iceni en Bretagne, elle mena une révolte majeure contre l'Empire romain au Ier siècle. Devenue célèbre pour avoir uni plusieurs tribus contre l'oppresseur, elle détruisit des villes romaines avant d'être vaincue. Son courage et sa résistance en ont fait une figure emblématique de l'histoire britannique.
summary.en: A Celtic queen of the Iceni tribe in ancient Britain, she led a major uprising against the Roman Empire in AD 60-61. Known for uniting tribes and destroying Roman settlements, her rebellion remains one of the most significant challenges to Roman rule. Her defiance made her a legendary symbol of British resistance.

name: Hatshepsut
facts: Became the fifth pharaoh of the Eighteenth Dynasty of Egypt, reigning from approximately 1478 to 1458 BCE.
Is one of the few and most successful female pharaohs in ancient Egyptian history.
Initially served as regent for her stepson, Thutmose III, before assuming full pharaonic powers.
Adopted the full titles and regalia of a pharaoh, including the false beard symbolizing kingship.
Commissioned extensive building projects, including the famous mortuary temple at Deir el-Bahari.
Oversaw a prosperous and peaceful reign, focusing on trade and economic development rather than military conquests.
Organized a significant trading expedition to the land of Punt, bringing back gold, myrrh, and exotic plants.
Constructed and restored numerous temples and monuments throughout Egypt, including at Karnak.
Depicted herself in statues and reliefs as both a woman and a male pharaoh to assert her authority.
Her reign saw advancements in art, architecture, and religious practices in ancient Egypt.
After her death, many of her monuments and inscriptions were defaced or destroyed, likely by Thutmose III or his supporters.
Rediscovered in modern times, her legacy has been reassessed, highlighting her political acumen and leadership.
Ruled during a time when Egypt's influence and wealth were at their peak in the New Kingdom period.
Her mummy was identified in 2007 through DNA testing and forensic analysis.
Celebrated for her diplomatic skills and ability to maintain stability and prosperity in Egypt.

summary.fr: Je suis une des rares femmes à avoir régné comme pharaon dans l'Égypte antique, vers 1478-1458 av. J.-C. J'ai marqué l'histoire en gouvernant avec succès et en lançant de grands projets architecturaux, comme le temple de Deir el-Bahari. Mon règne pacifique a favorisé le commerce et la prospérité, notamment grâce à une expédition célèbre vers le pays de Pount.
summary.en: I was one of the few and most successful female pharaohs in ancient Egypt, ruling from around 1478 to 1458 BCE. Known for my political skill, I oversaw major building projects like the mortuary temple at Deir el-Bahari and promoted trade during a peaceful reign. My legacy was later erased but has since been rediscovered and celebrated.

name: Saladin
facts: Founded the Ayyubid dynasty, which ruled over Egypt, Syria, Yemen, and parts of Mesopotamia and the Hijaz.
Born in 1137 in Tikrit, modern-day Iraq, to a prominent Kurdish family.
Became the vizier of Egypt in 1169 under the Fatimid Caliphate before consolidating power and abolishing the Fatimid dynasty in 1171.
Proclaimed himself Sultan of Egypt in 1174, marking the beginning of Ayyubid rule.
Led Muslim forces during the Crusades and recaptured Jerusalem from the Crusaders in 1187 after the Battle of Hattin.
Known for his chivalry and magnanimity, famously allowing Christian civilians to leave Jerusalem unharmed after its capture.
Established a reputation for generosity and justice, often distributing wealth to the poor and funding public works.
Fought against the Third Crusade, led by Richard the Lionheart, resulting in the Treaty of Ramla in 1192, which allowed Christian pilgrims access to Jerusalem.
Died in Damascus in 1193, reportedly leaving behind little personal wealth due to his charitable donations.
Unified much of the Muslim world under his leadership, fostering a period of relative stability and cultural flourishing.
Commissioned the construction of the Citadel of Cairo and expanded the city’s fortifications.
Studied Islamic theology, law, and military strategy from a young age, shaping his leadership style.
His name, Salah ad-Din, means 'Righteousness of the Faith' in Arabic.
Often depicted in European medieval literature as a noble and honorable adversary, contrasting with other Muslim leaders of the time.
His legacy endures as a symbol of resistance against foreign occupation and a model of Islamic leadership.

image.commons_file: Al-Nasir I Salah al-Din Yusuf (Saladin). AH 564-589 (1169-1193 CE) Æ Dirham (30.1mm, 13.28 g, 6h). Without mint-name. Dated AH 586 (AD 1190-91). Sultan sitting facing, cross-legged, on high-backed throne (obverse).jpg
image.author: Classical Numismatic Group (CNG)

summary.fr: Ce leader kurde du XIIe siècle a marqué l'histoire en fondant la dynastie ayyoubide et en unifiant une grande partie du monde musulman. Il est surtout célèbre pour avoir repris Jérusalem aux croisés en 1187 et pour sa réputation de clémence envers ses ennemis. Stratège militaire et homme de pouvoir, il a aussi laissé l'image d'un dirigeant généreux et juste.
summary.en: This 12th-century Kurdish leader made history by founding the Ayyubid dynasty and uniting much of the Muslim world. He is best known for recapturing Jerusalem from the Crusaders in 1187 and for his reputation for chivalry and mercy. A skilled military strategist and ruler, he was also admired for his generosity and sense of justice.

name: Confucius
facts: Born in 551 BCE in the state of Lu, in what is now Qufu, Shandong Province, China.
Founded Confucianism, a philosophical and ethical system that deeply influenced East Asian culture and thought.
Emphasized personal and governmental morality, correctness of social relationships, and justice.
Advocated for the concept of 'Ren' (benevolence or humaneness) as the foundation of ethical behavior.
Developed the Golden Rule: 'Do not do to others what you do not want done to yourself.'
Served as a government official in the state of Lu, holding positions such as Minister of Crime.
Traveled extensively across various Chinese states, seeking to advise rulers on governance and ethics.
Compiled and edited classical Chinese texts, including the 'Five Classics,' which became central to Confucian education.
His teachings were later collected by his disciples in the 'Analects,' a foundational Confucian text.
Died in 479 BCE, leaving behind a legacy that would shape Chinese civilization for millennia.
Posthumously honored with the title 'Supreme Sage and Teacher' during the Tang Dynasty.
Influenced later philosophers such as Mencius and Xunzi, who expanded on his ideas.
Confucian temples were built in his honor, with the most famous located in Qufu.
His philosophy became the official state ideology of China during the Han Dynasty (206 BCE–220 CE).
Remains a symbol of Chinese culture and is revered as one of the greatest thinkers in history.

image.commons_file: Confucius, fresco from a Western Han tomb of Dongping County, Shandong province, China.jpg
image.author: Anonymous Chinese painter of the Western Han period

summary.fr: Ce philosophe chinois du VIe siècle avant J.-C. a fondé le confucianisme, un système éthique et moral qui a profondément marqué la culture est-asiatique. Il a enseigné l'importance de la bienveillance, de la justice et des relations sociales harmonieuses, influençant des siècles de pensée et de gouvernance en Chine.
summary.en: This 6th-century BCE Chinese philosopher founded Confucianism, an ethical system that shaped East Asian culture and governance. He emphasized morality, social harmony, and benevolence, leaving a lasting legacy through works like the Analects and influencing generations of thinkers.

name: Avicenna
facts: Born in 980 CE in Afshana, near Bukhara, in present-day Uzbekistan.
Memorized the Quran by the age of 10 and studied Islamic jurisprudence, philosophy, and natural sciences by 14.
Wrote the *Canon of Medicine*, a five-volume encyclopedia that became a standard medical text in Europe and the Islamic world for centuries.
Composed over 450 works, with approximately 240 surviving, covering topics like medicine, philosophy, astronomy, and chemistry.
Served as a physician and advisor to multiple Persian rulers, including the Samanid and Buyid dynasties.
Developed the concept of contagion and the spread of diseases through water and soil, centuries before germ theory.
Described the symptoms and treatment of meningitis, diabetes, and other diseases with remarkable accuracy for his time.
Wrote *The Book of Healing*, a vast philosophical and scientific encyclopedia addressing logic, metaphysics, and natural sciences.
Introduced the idea of clinical trials and experimental medicine, emphasizing observation and empirical evidence.
Invented the process of steam distillation, which is still used today to produce essential oils.
Diagnosed and treated his own illnesses, including a severe case of colic, through self-prescribed remedies.
Died in 1037 CE in Hamadan, Persia, after a long illness, possibly due to overwork or poisoning.
His works were translated into Latin in the 12th century and influenced European scholars like Thomas Aquinas and Roger Bacon.
The *Canon of Medicine* was used as a textbook in European universities until the 17th century.
Considered one of the most influential polymaths in history, bridging the gap between ancient Greek and Islamic scholarship.

image.commons_file: Avicenna Portrait on Silver Vase - Museum at BuAli Sina (Avicenna) Mausoleum - Hamadan - Western Iran (7423560860).jpg
image.author: Adam Jones from Kelowna, BC, Canada

summary.fr: Ce savant persan du Xe siècle est l'un des plus grands médecins et philosophes de l'histoire. Il a écrit le *Canon de Médecine*, un ouvrage majeur qui a influencé la médecine en Europe et dans le monde islamique pendant des siècles. Ses travaux ont aussi posé les bases de la médecine expérimentale et de la distillation moderne.
summary.en: This 10th-century Persian scholar was one of history’s greatest physicians and philosophers. He authored the *Canon of Medicine*, a foundational work that shaped medical practice in Europe and the Islamic world for centuries. His contributions also advanced experimental medicine and the process of steam distillation.

name: Grace Hopper
facts: Was a pioneer in computer programming and one of the first programmers of the Harvard Mark I computer in 1944.
Developed the first compiler for a computer programming language, known as the A-0 system, in 1952.
Played a key role in the development of COBOL, one of the first high-level programming languages, in the late 1950s.
Coined the term 'debugging' after removing an actual moth from a computer in 1947, which is often cited as the first literal computer bug.
Earned a Ph.D. in mathematics from Yale University in 1934, one of the few women to do so at the time.
Served in the U.S. Navy Reserve, rising to the rank of Rear Admiral before retiring in 1986 at the age of 79.
Received the National Medal of Technology in 1991, becoming the first individual woman to receive the honor.
Was posthumously awarded the Presidential Medal of Freedom by President Barack Obama in 2016.
Taught mathematics at Vassar College before joining the Navy during World War II.
Popularized the idea of machine-independent programming languages, which led to the development of high-level languages.
Known for her motivational speeches, often using a nanosecond visualized as a length of wire to explain computer concepts.
The Grace Hopper Celebration of Women in Computing, the world's largest gathering of women technologists, is named in her honor.
Worked on the UNIVAC I, the first commercial computer produced in the United States, in the early 1950s.
Advocated for the standardization of programming languages across different computers, a concept that became widely adopted.

summary.fr: Cette scientifique américaine a marqué l'histoire de l'informatique en créant l'un des premiers compilateurs et en participant au développement du langage COBOL. Elle a aussi servi dans la marine américaine et popularisé le terme 'débogage' après avoir retiré un vrai papillon d'un ordinateur. Une pionnière qui a inspiré des générations de femmes dans les technologies.
summary.en: This American computer scientist pioneered programming by developing one of the first compilers and co-creating COBOL, a foundational programming language. She served in the U.S. Navy, coined the term 'debugging,' and became a role model for women in technology, earning prestigious awards for her groundbreaking work.

name: Hedy Lamarr
aliases: The Most Beautiful Woman in Films
facts: Born Hedwig Eva Maria Kiesler on November 9, 1914, in Vienna, Austria-Hungary.
Developed an early interest in acting and technology, studying both in her youth.
Starred in the controversial 1933 Czech film *Ecstasy*, which featured one of the first on-screen depictions of a female orgasm.
Fled Austria in 1937 to escape her controlling husband, Friedrich Mandl, a wealthy arms dealer with ties to fascist regimes.
Married MGM studio head Louis B. Mayer in 1938 and adopted the stage name Hedy Lamarr.
Became a Hollywood star, appearing in films like *Algiers* (1938), *Boom Town* (1940), and *Samson and Delilah* (1949).
Co-invented an early version of frequency-hopping spread spectrum technology during World War II with composer George Antheil.
Filed a patent in 1942 for a
Secret Communication System
designed to prevent enemy jamming of radio-controlled torpedoes.
The frequency-hopping technology laid the groundwork for modern Wi-Fi, GPS, and Bluetooth communication systems.
Received the Electronic Frontier Foundation Pioneer Award in 1997 for her contributions to technology.
Inducted into the National Inventors Hall of Fame in 2014, decades after her invention was recognized as groundbreaking.
Struggled with financial difficulties later in life despite her earlier fame and success.
Published an autobiography titled *Ecstasy and Me* in 1966, which was later criticized for inaccuracies.
Passed away on January 19, 2000, in Casselberry, Florida, at the age of 85.
Posthumously awarded a star on the Hollywood Walk of Fame in 2014 for her contributions to the film industry.

image.commons_file: Hedy Lamarr Publicity Photo for The Heavenly Body 1944.jpg
image.author: Unknown authorUnknown author

summary.fr: Hedy Lamarr était une actrice autrichienne-américaine célèbre à Hollywood dans les années 1940. En plus de son talent pour le cinéma, elle a co-inventé une technologie révolutionnaire pendant la Seconde Guerre mondiale, qui a permis le développement du Wi-Fi et du GPS. Son génie scientifique est souvent méconnu malgré son impact majeur sur les communications modernes.
summary.en: Hedy Lamarr was an Austrian-American actress who became a Hollywood star in the 1940s. Beyond her film career, she co-invented a groundbreaking frequency-hopping technology during World War II, which later enabled modern Wi-Fi, GPS, and Bluetooth. Her scientific contributions remain underappreciated despite their lasting impact on technology.

name: Rachel Carson
facts: Born on May 27, 1907, in Springdale, Pennsylvania, and developed a love for nature and writing at an early age.
Graduated magna cum laude from Pennsylvania College for Women (now Chatham University) in 1929 with a degree in biology.
Earned a master's degree in zoology from Johns Hopkins University in 1932 while supporting her family during the Great Depression.
Joined the U.S. Bureau of Fisheries (later the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service) in 1936 as a junior aquatic biologist, becoming only the second woman hired for a full-time professional position.
Wrote radio scripts for the Bureau of Fisheries under the series *Romance Under the Waters*, which later evolved into her first book, *Under the Sea-Wind* (1941).
Published *The Sea Around Us* in 1951, which became a bestseller, won the National Book Award, and established her as a leading science writer.
Resigned from the Fish and Wildlife Service in 1952 to focus on writing full-time after the success of *The Sea Around Us*.
Spent four years researching the environmental impacts of pesticides, particularly DDT, leading to the publication of *Silent Spring* in 1962.
*Silent Spring* sparked a national debate on pesticide use, leading to the eventual ban of DDT in the U.S. and the creation of the Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) in 1970.
Faced intense criticism and personal attacks from the chemical industry and some government officials for challenging pesticide practices in *Silent Spring*.
Testified before Congress in 1963, calling for new policies to protect human health and the environment from chemical pollutants.
Awarded the Presidential Medal of Freedom posthumously in 1980 by President Jimmy Carter for her contributions to environmental conservation.
Diagnosed with breast cancer in 1957, which she battled privately while continuing her research and writing until her death on April 14, 1964.
Inspired the modern environmental movement, with her work influencing the establishment of Earth Day and numerous environmental organizations.
The Rachel Carson National Wildlife Refuge in Maine was established in 1966 to honor her legacy and protect coastal ecosystems.

summary.fr: Cette scientifique américaine du XXe siècle a révolutionné notre compréhension de l'environnement. Spécialiste de la biologie marine, elle a alerté le monde sur les dangers des pesticides dans son livre *Silent Spring*. Son travail a inspiré la création de l'Agence de protection de l'environnement aux États-Unis.
summary.en: This 20th-century American scientist transformed our understanding of the environment. A marine biologist, she exposed the dangers of pesticides in her book *Silent Spring*. Her work led to the creation of the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency and shaped modern environmental policy.

name: Amelia Earhart
facts: Was born on July 24, 1897, in Atchison, Kansas, to Samuel
Became the 16th woman to be issued a pilot's license by the Fédération Aéronautique Internationale (FAI) in 1923.
Set a women's world altitude record of 14,000 feet in 1922.
Was the first woman to fly solo across the Atlantic Ocean on May 20-21, 1932, completing the flight in 14 hours and 56 minutes.
Received the Distinguished Flying Cross from the U.S. Congress for being the first woman to fly solo across the Atlantic.
Wrote best-selling books about her flying experiences, including '20 Hrs., 40 Min.' and 'The Fun of It'.
Helped form The Ninety-Nines, an organization for female pilots, in 1929.
Became a visiting faculty member at Purdue University as an advisor to aeronautical engineering and a career counselor for women students in 1935.
Attempted to become the first woman to fly around the world in 1937, with navigator Fred Noonan.
Disappeared over the central Pacific Ocean near Howland Island on July 2, 1937, during her world flight attempt.
Was declared legally dead on January 5, 1939, after an extensive search failed to find any trace of her or her aircraft.
Flew a Lockheed Electra 10E, specially equipped for her world flight attempt.
Was a strong advocate for women's rights and encouraged women to pursue careers in male-dominated fields.
Received the Gold Medal from the National Geographic Society, presented by President Herbert Hoover.
Had a friendship with First Lady Eleanor Roosevelt, who also took flying lessons inspired by her.

summary.fr: Cette pionnière américaine de l'aviation est née à la fin du XIXe siècle. Elle a marqué l'histoire en devenant la première femme à traverser l'océan Atlantique en solitaire en 1932. Son mystérieux disparition lors d'un tour du monde en avion en 1937 reste célèbre dans le monde entier.
summary.en: This American aviation pioneer was born in the late 19th century. She made history as the first woman to fly solo across the Atlantic Ocean in 1932. Her mysterious disappearance during a 1937 world flight attempt remains globally renowned.

name: Nikita Khrushchev
facts: Served as the First Secretary of the Communist Party of the Soviet Union from 1953 to 1964.
Led the Soviet Union during part of the Cold War, succeeding Joseph Stalin.
Delivered the famous 'Secret Speech' in 1956, denouncing Stalin's cult of personality and purges.
Initiated the process of de-Stalinization, which aimed to reform the Soviet political system.
Oversaw the Soviet space program, including the launch of Sputnik in 1957 and Yuri Gagarin's spaceflight in 1961.
Engaged in the Cuban Missile Crisis in 1962, bringing the world to the brink of nuclear war.
Promoted agricultural reforms, including the Virgin Lands Campaign, which had mixed success.
Was known for his colorful and often undiplomatic rhetoric, including banging his shoe at the United Nations in 1960.
Authorized the construction of the Berlin Wall in 1961 to stem the flow of refugees from East to West Germany.
Faced internal party opposition and was removed from power in 1964 in a coup led by Leonid Brezhnev.
Born on April 15, 1894, in Kalinovka, a village in the Russian Empire, to a peasant family.
Worked as a metalworker and miner before rising through the ranks of the Communist Party.
Played a key role in the Battle of Stalingrad during World War II as a political commissar.
Wrote a memoir titled 'Khrushchev Remembers,' published posthumously in 1970, detailing his experiences in power.
Died of a heart attack on September 11, 1971, in Moscow, and was buried in the Novodevichy Cemetery.

summary.fr: Ce dirigeant soviétique a dirigé l'URSS pendant une partie de la Guerre froide après la mort de Staline. Il est connu pour avoir dénoncé les crimes de Staline lors du 'discours secret' de 1956 et pour avoir lancé le programme spatial soviétique. Son rôle dans la crise des missiles de Cuba a marqué l'histoire par son intensité dramatique.
summary.en: This Soviet leader governed the USSR during part of the Cold War after Stalin’s death. He is famous for denouncing Stalin’s crimes in the 1956 Secret Speech and for advancing the Soviet space program. His role in the Cuban Missile Crisis brought the world to the edge of nuclear conflict.

name: Golda Meir
facts: Born on May 3, 1898, in Kiev, Russian Empire (now Ukraine) to a poor Jewish family.
Immigrated to the United States in 1906 with her family, settling in Milwaukee, Wisconsin.
Became a teacher after graduating from the Milwaukee State Normal School in 1917.
Joined the Labor Zionist movement and moved to British Mandate Palestine in 1921 with her husband, Morris Myerson.
Worked in various roles within the Histadrut, the Israeli labor union, including as head of its political department.
Served as Israel's ambassador to the Soviet Union from 1948 to 1949, shortly after the country's founding.
Changed her surname from Myerson to Meir in 1956 at the request of then-Prime Minister David Ben-Gurion.
Became Israel's first female Minister of Labor and National Insurance in 1949, holding the position until 1956.
Appointed as Israel's Minister of Foreign Affairs in 1956, a role she held until 1966.
Played a key role in securing arms and support from France and other Western nations during the 1950s and 1960s.
Became the fourth Prime Minister of Israel on March 17, 1969, following the death of Levi Eshkol.
Led Israel during the Yom Kippur War in 1973, which began with a surprise attack by Egypt and Syria.
Resigned as Prime Minister in 1974, citing health reasons and the political fallout from the Yom Kippur War.
Known for her strong, no-nonsense leadership style and was often referred to as the 'Iron Lady' of Israeli politics.
Published her autobiography, *My Life*, in 1975, detailing her experiences in politics and diplomacy.

summary.fr: Née en 1898 dans une famille juive pauvre, cette femme politique a marqué l'histoire en devenant la première femme Premier ministre d'Israël en 1969. Elle a joué un rôle clé dans la diplomatie et la défense du pays, notamment pendant la guerre du Kippour en 1973. Son parcours, de l'immigration aux États-Unis à la création d'Israël, illustre son engagement pour le sionisme et la politique internationale.
summary.en: Born in 1898 to a poor Jewish family, this political leader made history as Israel's first female Prime Minister in 1969. She was instrumental in diplomacy and national security, especially during the 1973 Yom Kippur War. Her journey from immigrating to the U.S. to helping establish Israel reflects her dedication to Zionism and global politics.
